# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Capacitive Sensor Oscillator Frequency

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** A capacitive soil moisture sensor uses a 555-timer-based astable oscillator. The soil capacitance forms part of the timing RC network. Given: $R_A = 10\,k\Omega$, $R_B = 100\,k\Omega$, and the sensor capacitance $C_{soil}$ varies from 100 pF (dry) to 5 nF (saturated). Find the frequency range.

**Step 1 — Identify the 555 astable output frequency formula:**

$$
f = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) C_{soil}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values for the dry condition (C = 100 pF):**

$$
f_{dry} = \frac{1.44}{(10{,}000 + 2 \times 100{,}000) \times 100 \times 10^{-12}}
$$

$$
f_{dry} = \frac{1.44}{(210{,}000) \times 10^{-10}}
$$

$$
f_{dry} = \frac{1.44}{2.1 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
f_{dry} = 68{,}571.4 \;\text{Hz} \approx 68.6 \;\text{kHz}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute for the wet condition (C = 5 nF = 5 × 10⁻⁹ F):**

$$
f_{wet} = \frac{1.44}{(210{,}000) \times 5 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
f_{wet} = \frac{1.44}{1.05 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
f_{wet} = 1371.4 \;\text{Hz} \approx 1.37 \;\text{kHz}
$$

**Step 4 — Result:**

$$
f_{range} = 1.37 \;\text{kHz} \;\to\; 68.6 \;\text{kHz}
$$

This is a **50:1 frequency ratio**, which is easily measured by a microcontroller's 16-bit timer. The wide dynamic range is the engineering reason capacitive sensors are preferred for IoT-based precision agriculture.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: RTD Temperature from Resistance

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** A PT100 RTD reads R = 119.5 Ω. Find the temperature using the linear approximation. Given α = 3.85 × 10⁻³ °C⁻¹ and R₀ = 100 Ω.

**Step 1 — Apply the linear RTD equation:**

$$
R(T) = R_0 (1 + \alpha T)
$$

**Step 2 — Solve algebraically for T:**

$$
T = \frac{R(T) - R_0}{R_0 \cdot \alpha}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$
T = \frac{119.5 - 100}{100 \times 3.85 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
T = \frac{19.5}{0.385}
$$

$$
T = 50.65 \;°C
$$

**Step 4 — Verify with the Callendar-Van Dusen non-linear equation:**

For the 50 °C point, the higher-order term B T² is:

$$
B T^2 = (-5.775 \times 10^{-7}) \times (50)^2 = -1.444 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
R_{nonlin} = 100 \times (1 + 3.9083\!\times\!10^{-3} \times 50 - 1.444\!\times\!10^{-3})
$$

$$
R_{nonlin} = 100 \times (1 + 0.19542 - 0.001444) = 100 \times 1.19398 = 119.40 \;\Omega
$$

The **non-linear correction** is only ~0.08 % at 50 °C, confirming the linear approximation is valid up to ~150 °C.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: NTC Thermistor Temperature Using β-Equation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** An NTC thermistor has R₀ = 10 kΩ at T₀ = 25 °C = 298.15 K. The β-constant is 3950 K. The measured resistance is R = 4.5 kΩ. Find the temperature.

**Step 1 — Apply the β-equation:**

$$
R(T) = R_0 \, \exp\left[ B \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right) \right]
$$

**Step 2 — Take natural log of both sides:**

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{R}{R_0}\right) = B \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for 1/T:**

$$
\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{T_0} + \frac{1}{B} \ln\!\left(\frac{R}{R_0}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$
\frac{1}{T_0} = \frac{1}{298.15} = 3.354 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{K}^{-1}
$$

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{4500}{10000}\right) = \ln(0.45) = -0.7985
$$

$$
\frac{1}{B} = \frac{1}{3950} = 2.532 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{K}^{-1}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{B} \ln(0.45) = (2.532 \times 10^{-4}) \times (-0.7985) = -2.022 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 5 — Sum and invert:**

$$
\frac{1}{T} = 3.354 \times 10^{-3} - 2.022 \times 10^{-4} = 3.152 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{K}^{-1}
$$

$$
T = \frac{1}{3.152 \times 10^{-3}} = 317.3 \;\text{K}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to °C:**

$$
T_{°C} = 317.3 - 273.15 = 44.15 \;°C
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: pH from Nernst Equation (Two-Point Calibration)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** A glass-membrane pH electrode is calibrated at 25 °C using two standard buffer solutions: pH 4.01 (E₁ = 178 mV) and pH 6.86 (E₂ = −8 mV). An unknown soil sample gives E_x = 95 mV. Find the soil pH.

**Step 1 — Write the Nernst equation for the electrode at 25 °C:**

$$
E = E_0 - 0.05916 \cdot pH
$$

**Step 2 — Find the slope (theoretical should be −59.16 mV/pH, but real electrodes deviate — hence calibration):**

$$
\text{slope} = \frac{E_2 - E_1}{pH_2 - pH_1} = \frac{(-8) - 178}{6.86 - 4.01} = \frac{-186}{2.85} = -65.26 \;\text{mV/pH}
$$

**Step 3 — Find the intercept E₀:**

$$
E_0 = E_1 - (\text{slope}) \times pH_1 = 178 - (-65.26) \times 4.01 = 178 + 261.7 = 439.7 \;\text{mV}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute pH of unknown:**

$$
pH_x = \frac{E_x - E_0}{\text{slope}} = \frac{95 - 439.7}{-65.26} = \frac{-344.7}{-65.26} = 5.28
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:** pH 5.28 is **moderately acidic**. This is below the optimal 6.0–7.0 range; the farmer should apply **agricultural lime (CaCO₃)** to raise pH.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Temperature-Dependent Nernst Slope

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the 59.16 mV value?** Derivation: the Nernst slope is $2.303 \, RT / (zF)$.

**Step 1 — Plug in T = 298.15 K, R = 8.314 J/(mol·K), F = 96485 C/mol, z = 1:**

$$
\text{Slope} = \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298.15}{1 \times 96485}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute numerator:**

$$
2.303 \times 8.314 = 19.147
$$

$$
19.147 \times 298.15 = 5708.5
$$

**Step 3 — Divide by F:**

$$
\frac{5708.5}{96485} = 0.05916 \;\text{V/pH} = 59.16 \;\text{mV/pH}
$$

**Step 4 — At a different temperature, say 10 °C = 283.15 K:**

$$
\text{Slope}_{10°C} = \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 283.15}{96485} = 0.05618 \;\text{V/pH}
$$

**Step 5 — Consequence for KTU problems:** Always **temperature-compensate** the Nernst equation. A common engineering form is:

$$
E = E_0 - \frac{0.05916 \, T}{298.15} \cdot pH
$$

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]

**"What is a gypsum block soil moisture sensor? How does it work, and what is its main limitation?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **gypsum block sensor** is a porous CaSO₄·2H₂O block with two embedded electrodes. When buried in soil, water moves in or out of the porous matrix until hydraulic equilibrium with the surrounding soil is reached. The **electrical resistance** between the electrodes is inversely related to soil moisture (and directly related to soil matric potential). At field capacity the resistance is low (~0.2–5 kΩ); at the permanent wilting point the resistance is high (~50–200 kΩ).

**Main limitation:** Gypsum slowly **dissolves** in the soil (solubility ~2 g/L), causing calibration drift and a typical service life of only **1–3 years**. (1 mark limitation, 2 marks working).

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks (Module 1 Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **capacitive soil moisture sensor**. Derive the relationship between the sensor's oscillation frequency and soil dielectric constant. (7 marks)

**(b)** A capacitive sensor is used with a 555-timer astable circuit having $R_A = 5.6\,k\Omega$ and $R_B = 68\,k\Omega$. The capacitance varies from 200 pF (dry) to 4 nF (saturated). (i) Calculate the frequency range. (ii) If the microcontroller measures an output frequency of 8 kHz, estimate the volumetric water content using the **linearized dielectric model** $\theta = 0.05 + 0.005 \cdot \varepsilon_r$ (where the soil acts as the dielectric of a parallel-plate capacitor with A = 100 mm² and d = 2 mm). (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a) — 7 marks

**Block diagram (1.5 marks):** Refer to Section 4.2 — Interdigitated electrodes form a capacitor whose dielectric is the soil. This is the frequency-determining element of a 555 or NE555 astable oscillator. The output frequency is fed to a microcontroller.

**Working explanation (2.5 marks):**
1. The sensor has **interdigitated copper electrodes** on a PCB, forming a fringing-field capacitor.
2. The soil surrounding the electrodes acts as the **dielectric medium**.
3. Dry soil has $\varepsilon_r \approx 3$–5; wet soil has $\varepsilon_r \approx 15$–30 (effective); pure water has $\varepsilon_r \approx 80$.
4. The capacitance follows $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$.
5. The capacitance is part of an **LC/RC oscillator**: $f = 1 / (2\pi\sqrt{LC})$ for LC type, or $f = 1.44/((R_A + 2R_B)C)$ for 555 astable.
6. The microcontroller measures $f$ and computes $\varepsilon_r$, then maps it to VWC via calibration.

**Derivation (3 marks):** Starting from $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ and $f = 1 / (2\pi \sqrt{LC})$:

$$
f = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L \cdot (\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d)}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L \varepsilon_0 A / d}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,f = \frac{K}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}\,} \quad \text{where} \quad K = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{d}{L \varepsilon_0 A}}
$$

This proves that frequency is **inversely proportional to √(ε_r)**: as soil wetness increases, ε_r rises, and frequency falls.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b) — 7 marks

**Part (i) Frequency range (3 marks):**

Using $f = 1.44/((R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C)$:

**Dry condition (C = 200 pF):**

$$
f_{dry} = \frac{1.44}{(5600 + 2 \times 68000) \times 200 \times 10^{-12}}
$$

$$
R_A + 2R_B = 5{,}600 + 136{,}000 = 141{,}600\;\Omega
$$

$$
f_{dry} = \frac{1.44}{141{,}600 \times 2 \times 10^{-10}} = \frac{1.44}{2.832 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 50{,}847\;\text{Hz} \approx 50.8\;\text{kHz}
$$

**Wet condition (C = 4 nF):**

$$
f_{wet} = \frac{1.44}{141{,}600 \times 4 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{1.44}{5.664 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 2542\;\text{Hz} \approx 2.54\;\text{kHz}
$$

**Frequency range = 2.54 kHz to 50.8 kHz** (a 20:1 ratio).

**Part (ii) VWC at f = 8 kHz (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Solve for C from f:**

$$
C = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) \cdot f} = \frac{1.44}{141{,}600 \times 8000} = \frac{1.44}{1.133 \times 10^{9}} = 1.271 \times 10^{-9}\;\text{F} = 1.27\;\text{nF}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for ε_r:**

$$
\varepsilon_r = \frac{C \cdot d}{\varepsilon_0 \cdot A} = \frac{1.271 \times 10^{-9} \times 2 \times 10^{-3}}{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 100 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_r = \frac{2.542 \times 10^{-12}}{8.854 \times 10^{-16}} = 2871
$$

> [!WARNING]
> This unusually high ε_r signals that the linear capacitor model (with d = 2 mm) is invalid for **interdigitated fringing-field** sensors, where the effective geometry factor is different. The examiner expects a **datasheet or experimental calibration**, not a direct calculation. Always use the manufacturer's **ε_r–θ lookup table**. (This is the most common KTU mistake: using parallel-plate equations for interdigitated sensors.)

**Step 3 — Apply the given linear model formally:**

Assuming the fringing-field geometry gives an effective ε_r that maps through the calibration: for illustration, if we accept ε_r = 25 (a typical mid-range wet-soil value at 8 kHz with a calibrated geometry factor of ~0.01), then:

$$
\theta = 0.05 + 0.005 \times 25 = 0.05 + 0.125 = 0.175 = 17.5\%\;\text{VWC}
$$

This is within the **typical agronomic "moist" range** (15–25 % VWC).

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Module 1 Choice 2 — Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a **pH sensor** based on ion-selective electrodes. Derive the **Nernst equation** and write the simplified form valid at 25 °C. (7 marks)

**(b)** A soil pH probe is calibrated at 25 °C using two buffers:
- Buffer 1: pH 4.00 → E₁ = +170 mV
- Buffer 2: pH 9.00 → E₂ = −110 mV

A soil sample gives E_x = +30 mV. (i) Calculate the slope, intercept, and soil pH. (ii) If the temperature drops to 10 °C, what is the new theoretical Nernst slope, and what would be the corrected pH? (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a) — 7 marks

**Working Principle (3 marks):** A soil pH sensor consists of two electrodes:
1. **Glass membrane electrode (sensing)** — A thin Na₂O-CaO-SiO₂ glass bulb filled with 0.1 M HCl, with an internal Ag/AgCl wire.
2. **Reference electrode** — Ag/AgCl wire in saturated KCl, connected to the soil slurry through a porous ceramic frit (salt bridge).

When the glass bulb contacts the soil, a **phase-boundary potential** develops across the glass membrane, governed by the **ion-exchange equilibrium** of H⁺ ions with the alkali-metal ions in the glass. The potential difference between the inner and reference electrodes follows the Nernst equation.

**Derivation (4 marks):** Start from electrochemical equilibrium at the glass-solution interface. The Gibbs free energy change for transferring 1 mole of H⁺ across the membrane:

$$
\Delta G = -zFE
$$

At equilibrium with ion activity $a_{H^+}$ in the soil:

$$
E = E_0 + \frac{RT}{zF} \ln(a_{H^+})
$$

Since $pH = -\log_{10}(a_{H^+})$ and $\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x)$:

$$
E = E_0 - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{F} \cdot pH
$$

At T = 298.15 K:

$$
E = E_0 - 0.05916 \cdot pH \;\text{[V]}
$$

**Simplified form:** $\boxed{E = E_0 - 0.05916 \cdot pH}$ at 25 °C.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b) — 7 marks

**Part (i) — Calibration & soil pH at 25 °C (4 marks):**

**Slope:**

$$
\text{slope} = \frac{E_2 - E_1}{pH_2 - pH_1} = \frac{-110 - 170}{9.00 - 4.00} = \frac{-280}{5.00} = -56.0\;\text{mV/pH}
$$

**Intercept** (using point 1):

$$
E_0 = E_1 - \text{slope} \times pH_1 = 170 - (-56.0) \times 4.00 = 170 + 224 = 394\;\text{mV}
$$

**Soil pH at E_x = +30 mV:**

$$
pH_x = \frac{E_x - E_0}{\text{slope}} = \frac{30 - 394}{-56.0} = \frac{-364}{-56.0} = 6.50
$$

The soil is **slightly acidic**, ideal for most field crops.

**Part (ii) — Temperature correction at 10 °C (3 marks):**

The theoretical Nernst slope at 10 °C = 283.15 K:

$$
\text{Slope}_{10°C} = \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 283.15}{96485} = 0.05618\;\text{V/pH} = 56.18\;\text{mV/pH}
$$

Apply a temperature correction factor to the calibration. Since the empirical slope (56.0 mV/pH) was close to the theoretical at 25 °C, the corrected slope at 10 °C is still ~56.0 mV/pH (assuming the electrode's slope tracks the theoretical Nernst factor):

$$
pH_{corrected} = \frac{E_x - E_0 (T)}{\text{Slope}(T)} \approx \frac{30 - (394 \times 10/25\;\text{rough estimate})}{56.0}
$$

**More rigorously:** The intercept $E_0$ is also slightly temperature-dependent, but for a first-order correction we use only the slope change. Since the measured E_x = +30 mV was at 25 °C, the temperature drop to 10 °C affects the **future readings**, not this one. The corrected slope for the **next reading at 10 °C** would be 56.18 mV/pH — virtually identical to the empirical 56.0, confirming the electrode is operating near-Nernstian efficiency (about 95 %).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in KTU 2024 ESE for Soil Sensors:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up θ definitions:** Students write GWC (%) and VWC (%) interchangeably. They are **NOT the same**. GWC = mass of water / mass of dry soil; VWC = volume of water / total volume. Conversion requires bulk density: $\theta = GWC \times (\rho_b / \rho_w)$.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert °C to K in the Nernst equation** — losing 1–2 marks. Always convert 25 °C → 298.15 K before substituting.
> 3. **Using parallel-plate capacitor equation for interdigitated capacitive sensors** — they have a different geometry factor. Always check the datasheet's $\varepsilon_r$–VWC calibration chart.
> 4. **Sign error in the Nernst equation:** Some students write $E = E_0 + 0.05916 \cdot pH$ instead of the minus sign. Remember: **higher pH → more negative mV**.
> 5. **RTD lead-wire resistance:** For a 2-wire PT100, the lead-wire resistance (typically 0.1–2 Ω) **adds directly to the measured R**, causing errors of 0.3–6 °C. Always specify **3-wire or 4-wire (Kelvin) connection** in KTU answers.
> 6. **Thermocouple reference junction:** Students forget that the Seebeck voltage is **relative to the cold junction temperature**, not absolute. **CJC is mandatory** in any practical circuit.
> 7. **Gypsum block drift:** Failing to mention the **1–3 year lifespan** and the **calibration drift due to CaSO₄ dissolution** — this is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction in long answers.
> 8. **pH ISE high-impedance:** Glass-membrane electrodes have internal resistance of **50–500 MΩ**. They require a **high-impedance (>10¹² Ω) op-amp** input stage. Students who mention "amplifier" but not "high-impedance" lose marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist (KTU 2024 Module 1):**
>
> ✅ A **Soil Monitoring System (SMS)** integrates sensors, signal conditioning, DAQ, telemetry, and decision support — its goal is **precision agriculture**.
>
> ✅ **Volumetric Water Content (VWC, θ)** is the fundamental moisture metric: $\theta = V_{water}/V_{soil}$ in m³/m³ (0–55 %).
>
> ✅ **Capacitive sensors** exploit the **ε_water ≈ 80 vs ε_soil ≈ 4** contrast; they are **non-contact**, corrosion-free, and IoT-friendly. Output is **frequency** from a 555 oscillator.
>
> ✅ **Topp Equation** is the universal ε_a–θ calibration valid for mineral soils.
>
> ✅ **Thermocouples** use the **Seebeck effect**: $V \approx S \cdot \Delta T$. **Type K** (~40 μV/°C) is standard. **Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC)** is mandatory.
>
> ✅ **RTDs** use the **linear** resistance of platinum: $R = R_0(1 + \alpha T)$, with $\alpha_{Pt} = 3.85 \times 10^{-3}$ °C⁻¹. **PT100** = 100 Ω at 0 °C. **3-wire / 4-wire Kelvin** connections eliminate lead resistance.
>
> ✅ **Thermistors (NTC)** use the **β-equation**: $R(T) = R_0 \exp[B(1/T - 1/T_0)]$ with B ≈ 3000–5000 K. Highly sensitive but non-linear — use **Steinhart-Hart** for accuracy.
>
> ✅ **Gypsum block** measures **resistance vs. soil matric potential** ($\psi$). Cheap, emulates plant water stress, but **dissolves in 1–3 years**.
>
> ✅ **pH ISEs** use the **Nernst equation**: $E = E_0 - 0.05916 \cdot pH$ at 25 °C (z = +1). Slope is **59.16 mV/pH**, temperature-dependent.
>
> ✅ **Two-point calibration** with pH 4 and pH 7 (or pH 9) is mandatory for pH sensors.
>
> ✅ **Constants to memorize:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m; $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K); $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol; Nernst slope at 25 °C = 59.16 mV/pH.
>
> ✅ **Agronomic optimum ranges:** VWC = 15–35 %; soil T = 15–30 °C; pH = 6.0–7.0.
>
> ✅ **Sensor selection logic:** Capacitive for IoT; TDR for research; Gypsum for low-cost; Thermocouple for rugged wide range; RTD for accuracy; Thermistor for low-power IoT; pH ISE for nutrient management.
>
> ✅ **Engineering advantages of capacitive over resistive moisture sensors:** No DC current → no electrolytic corrosion → 5–10 year lifespan.
>
> ✅ **Nernst equation is temperature-dependent** — always include the $T$ factor for high-precision work; a 10 °C change shifts the slope by ~5 %.
>
> ✅ **Glass pH electrodes are high-impedance** (50–500 MΩ) and require electrometer-grade op-amps (e.g., LMP91200, ADS1292).

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Water Quality Parameters — The Four Pillars

Agricultural water quality is not judged by a single property; it is a **multi-parameter fingerprint** that determines whether water is fit for irrigation, livestock, aquaculture, and food processing. The four universally accepted primary parameters are **pH, Electrical Conductivity (EC), Dissolved Oxygen (DO), and Turbidity**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 2 (PEAET632)**
> The student must be able to define, distinguish, and explain the working principle of sensors for **pH, EC, DO, and Turbidity**, and connect each parameter to its agricultural consequence.

### 1.1.1 pH — The "Acidity vs Alkalinity" Meter

**Formal Definition:** pH is the **negative base-10 logarithm of the hydrogen ion (H⁺) activity** in an aqueous solution, mathematically expressed as:

$$\text{pH} = -\log_{10}[a_{H^+}]$$

Where $a_{H^+}$ is the activity of hydrogen ions in mol/L. The scale ranges from **0 (strongly acidic) to 14 (strongly alkaline)**, with **7.0** being neutral at 25 °C.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why pH matters in agriculture:** Most crops grow best in a soil-water pH range of **6.0 – 7.5**. Acidic water (pH < 5.5) leaches essential nutrients (Ca, Mg, K) and mobilises toxic Al³⁺ ions, while alkaline water (pH > 8.5) causes iron chlorosis and reduces the availability of phosphorus, zinc, and manganese.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Think of pH as a "loudness meter" for a chemical argument. Imagine a glass of lemonade (acidic, lots of H⁺ "shouting") and a glass of soapy water (alkaline, lots of OH⁻ "shouting"). Neutral water (pH 7) is a quiet room where neither side dominates. The logarithmic scale means **each unit of pH = 10× change in acidity** (pH 5 is ten times more acidic than pH 6).

### 1.1.2 Electrical Conductivity (EC) — The "Saltiness" Detector

**Formal Definition:** Electrical Conductivity is the measure of a solution's ability to conduct an electric current, which is directly proportional to the **total concentration of dissolved ionic salts** (TDS – Total Dissolved Solids). The SI unit is **Siemens per metre (S/m)**, but in agriculture it is universally reported in **milliSiemens per centimetre (mS/cm)** or **dS/m** (deciSiemens per metre).

$$\text{EC} \;\propto\; \text{TDS} \approx \text{Sum of cations (Na⁺, Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, K⁺) + anions (Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻, HCO₃⁻)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Salinity Classification (USDA Standard):**
> - **< 0.25 dS/m** — Non-saline (Excellent for irrigation)
> - **0.25 – 0.75 dS/m** — Slightly saline (Suitable for most crops)
> - **0.75 – 2.25 dS/m** — Moderately saline (Restrictive for sensitive crops)
> - **> 2.25 dS/m** — Highly saline (Severely limits yield)

**Intuitive Analogy:** Pour table salt (NaCl) into a glass of distilled water and it stops conducting electricity well — it actually starts conducting **better**! That's because the Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions become mobile charge carriers. Pure distilled water has EC near **0.005 mS/cm**; seawater has EC around **50 mS/cm**. In agriculture, EC is a quick proxy for the "salt stress" a plant will experience.

### 1.1.3 Dissolved Oxygen (DO) — The "Underwater Breath" Gauge

**Formal Definition:** Dissolved Oxygen is the amount of **molecular oxygen (O₂) dissolved in water**, expressed in **mg/L (ppm)** or as **% saturation**. It is governed by Henry's Law and varies inversely with temperature and salinity.

$$\text{DO}_{saturation} = f(T, P, \text{Salinity})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical DO thresholds for aquaculture & irrigation:**
> - **< 2 mg/L** — Hypoxic (fish kill zone)
> - **2 – 4 mg/L** — Stressful for aquatic life
> - **5 – 8 mg/L** — Healthy range for aquaculture
> - **> 8 mg/L** — Supersaturation (gas bubble disease risk)

**Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a goldfish in a sealed jar. The fish "breathes" O₂ dissolved in the water, just as we breathe atmospheric O₂. If the water is warm, salty, or stagnant, the water "holds" less O₂ — equivalent to a fish living on a mountaintop with thin air. Cold, moving, well-aerated water is the "fresh air" of the underwater world.

### 1.1.4 Turbidity — The "Cloudiness" Meter

**Formal Definition:** Turbidity is an **optical property** that indicates the presence of suspended and colloidal matter (clay, silt, organic matter, algae, microorganisms) in water. It is measured by detecting the **scattering of light** (typically at 90° to the incident beam) and expressed in **Nephelometric Turbidity Units (NTU)**.

$$\tau = -\frac{1}{L} \ln\!\left(\frac{I_{transmitted}}{I_{incident}}\right)$$

Where $\tau$ is the turbidity coefficient and $L$ is the path length of the light beam.

> [!NOTE]
> **NTU Classification (WHO/USEPA):**
> - **< 1 NTU** — Drinking water standard
> - **1 – 5 NTU** — Acceptable for irrigation
> - **5 – 50 NTU** — Cloudy; may clog drip emitters
> - **> 50 NTU** — Highly turbid; unsuitable without filtration

**Intuitive Analogy:** Hold a torch in a clean swimming pool — you can see the beam clearly because the water is full of suspended dust/chlorine particles that scatter the light. In a perfectly clean aquarium, the beam is nearly invisible. Turbidity is essentially measuring "how visibly messy" the water is.

---

## 1.2 Basic Principles of Water Quality Sensors

A water quality sensor is a **transducer** that converts a chemical/physical property of water into a measurable electrical signal (voltage, current, frequency, or impedance).

| Sensor Type | Physical / Chemical Principle | Output Signal |
|-------------|------------------------------|---------------|
| **pH Electrode** | Glass-membrane H⁺ ion-selective potential (Nernst equation) | mV (high-impedance) |
| **EC Probe** | 4-electrode conductivity cell (Kelvin) | mS/cm |
| **DO Probe** | Polarographic Clark-type (Au cathode + Ag/AgCl anode) | nA to µA |
| **Turbidity Sensor** | 90° light scatter (Nephelometry, 860 nm IR LED) | mV / NTU |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Nernst Equation — Heart of the pH Sensor:**
> $$E = E_0 - \frac{2.303\,RT}{nF} \log[a_{H^+}] = E_0 + \frac{0.0592\,V}{n} \cdot \text{pH}\quad\text{(at 25°C)}$$
> A perfectly working pH probe gives **59.16 mV per pH unit** at 25 °C. This slope is the single most important number checked during pH probe calibration.

---

## 1.3 Irrigation Systems — Necessity, Methods, and Hierarchy

### 1.3.1 Why Irrigate?

**Necessity of Irrigation:**
1. **Rainfall uncertainty** — Monsoon failure, drought years.
2. **Crop water demand** — Modern HYV (High-Yielding Variety) crops transpire far more water than traditional cultivars.
3. **Soil moisture control** — Maintains the field at **Field Capacity (FC)** rather than drying to **Permanent Wilting Point (PWP)**.
4. **Year-round farming** — Rabi/summer crops are wholly dependent on stored/irrigated water.
5. **Salt leaching** — Periodic heavy irrigation flushes accumulated salts below the root zone.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition:** Irrigation is the **artificial application of water to soil** for the purpose of supplying the moisture essential for plant growth. It is a *supplement* to rainfall, never a *substitute* for proper drainage and agronomy.

### 1.3.2 Classification of Irrigation Methods

| Category | Methods | WUE Range |
|----------|---------|-----------|
| **Surface (Gravity)** | Flood, Furrow, Basin, Border | 30 – 50 % |
| **Sprinkler (Overhead)** | Portable, Semi-permanent, Centre Pivot, Lateral Move, Big Gun | 60 – 75 % |
| **Drip / Micro** | Surface drip, Sub-surface drip, Micro-sprinkler, Bubbler | 80 – 95 % |
| **Subsurface** | Sub-irrigation, subirr. drainage control | 70 – 85 % |

> WUE = Water Use Efficiency

**Intuitive Analogy:** Irrigation methods are like ways to water a houseplant. You can flood the entire pot (flood irrigation, wasteful), spray from above like a shower (sprinkler), or feed water drop-by-drop directly to the root zone through a tube (drip) — the latter is by far the most efficient because almost no water is "wasted" on the surrounding air or soil.

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deep Dive — The Four Water Quality Parameters

### 2.1.1 pH — Glass Membrane Electrode Theory

**Working principle (step-by-step):**

1. The pH probe has a **thin glass membrane** at its tip, sensitive only to H⁺ ions.
2. Two solutions exist: the **external unknown sample** and an **internal reference (0.1 M HCl)**.
3. H⁺ ions selectively diffuse into the hydrated gel layer of the glass.
4. A **potential difference (E)** develops across the membrane, following the **Nernst equation**.
5. The high-impedance mV signal is amplified and converted to a pH reading.

**Why the glass is selective to H⁺:**
The glass membrane is composed of SiO₂ + Na₂O + CaO. The Na⁺ and Ca²⁺ ions act as **ionic charge carriers** that are exchanged only for H⁺ from the solution. Li⁺, Na⁺, K⁺ ions do not penetrate — this is the *selectivity coefficient* principle.

### 2.1.2 EC — The 4-Electrode (Kelvin) Cell

A 4-electrode EC probe overcomes the polarisation errors of a 2-electrode cell:

- **Two outer electrodes (C1, C2)** — pass an AC current through the water.
- **Two inner electrodes (P1, P2)** — measure the resulting voltage drop.
- The ratio V/I gives the **cell constant (K)**:
$$K = \frac{l}{A} \quad \text{(cm}^{-1}\text{)}$$

Where $l$ = distance between voltage electrodes, $A$ = cross-sectional area of the current path. The actual conductivity is:

$$\kappa = \frac{K}{R} \quad \text{(S/cm)}$$

> **Why AC is used:** Direct current causes ion migration and polarisation, fouling the electrodes. AC (typically 1 kHz) prevents this, giving a stable, drift-free reading.

### 2.1.3 DO — Polarographic Clark Sensor

**Construction:**
- **Au cathode** (small) — where O₂ is reduced.
- **Ag/AgCl anode** (large) — where the counter-reaction occurs.
- **Teflon/PFTE gas-permeable membrane** — separates electrolyte (KCl) from sample.
- A polarising voltage of **~0.7 V** is applied.

**Half-reactions:**
- At cathode: $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$
- At anode: $4Ag + 4Cl^- \rightarrow 4AgCl + 4e^-$

The current is **directly proportional to DO concentration** (Fick's first law of diffusion through the membrane).

### 2.1.4 Turbidity — Nephelometric Sensor

**Optical geometry:**
- **Light source:** IR LED at 860 nm (minimises colour interference from dissolved substances).
- **Detector:** Silicon photodiode at **90°** to the incident beam.
- **Reference detector:** Monitors LED ageing and compensates intensity drift.
- The detector receives **scattered light intensity** $I_s$ proportional to particle concentration.

$$I_s \propto N \cdot V_p^2 \cdot I_0$$

Where $N$ = number of particles, $V_p$ = particle volume, $I_0$ = incident intensity. (Mie scattering theory for larger particles; Rayleigh for sub-µm).

---

## 2.2 Irrigation Systems — Engineering Theory

### 2.2.1 Overhead / Sprinkler Irrigation

**Components:**
1. **Pump unit** (centrifugal or submersible)
2. **Pipe network** (main, sub-main, laterals — aluminium/PVC/HDPE)
3. **Sprinkler nozzle** (single-nozzle, twin-nozzle, or rotating impact)
4. **Couplers, valves, pressure regulators**

**Operating principle:** Pressurised water (2 – 4 kg/cm²) is sprayed as fine droplets that mimic rainfall. Sprinkler discharge is calculated as:

$$q = C_d \cdot A_n \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$$

Where $C_d$ = discharge coefficient (~0.95), $A_n$ = nozzle area, $H$ = operating pressure head.

> [!NOTE]
> **Application rate** of sprinkler must be **less than the soil's infiltration rate**, otherwise surface ponding and runoff occur:
> $$I_{sprinkler} \le I_{soil,infiltration}$$

### 2.2.2 Centre Pivot Irrigation

- A **single long lateral pipe** (often 200 – 800 m) rotates about a fixed central pivot.
- Supported on **mobile towers** (spans of 30 – 65 m each).
- Sprinkler/nozzle packages hang below the lateral.
- **End gun** at the far end extends radius.
- The lateral moves in a **circular pattern**; angular velocity is controlled by the outermost tower.
- **Area covered** of a full circle: $A = \pi R^2$.
- Field corners (square fields) can be irrigated using a **corner attachment (swing arm)**.

> **Intuition:** A giant rotating garden sprinkler, but at field scale.

### 2.2.3 Lateral Move (Linear Move) Irrigation

- Similar to centre pivot, but the **lateral moves in a straight line** across a rectangular field.
- A **water supply channel** runs along one field edge, and the lateral is fed by a **flexible hose** or **drag shoe**.
- Higher land-use efficiency (~98 %) compared to centre pivot (~78 % for square fields).
- Power is supplied by a **diesel generator** mounted on the cart.

### 2.2.4 Micro Irrigation (Drip)

**Sub-classes:**
- **Surface drip** — laterals laid on soil surface.
- **Sub-surface drip (SDI)** — buried 10 – 30 cm deep.
- **Micro-sprinkler** — low-pressure fine spray.
- **Bubbler** — floods a small basin (5 – 50 L/min).
- **Micro-jet** — fine spray at low pressure (1 – 2 bar).

**Components of a drip system:**
1. **Head control unit** (pump, filter, fertiliser tank, pressure gauge, flow meter, control valve).
2. **Main and sub-main lines** (PVC).
3. **Laterals** (LDPE 12 mm, 16 mm, 20 mm).
4. **Drippers/emitters** (0.5 – 8 L/h; online or inline).
5. **Filters** (screen, disc, sand, hydrocyclone).
6. **Pressure regulators** and **air/vacuum relief valves**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Emitter classification** is a KTU-favourite sub-topic:
> - **Pressure-compensating (PC)** — maintains constant discharge over a pressure range.
> - **Non-pressure-compensating (NPC)** — discharge varies with pressure.
> - **Self-flushing** — automatically purges sediment.

The fundamental emitter discharge equation:

$$q = K \cdot H^x$$

Where $q$ = discharge, $K$ = emitter constant, $H$ = pressure head, $x$ = emitter exponent.
- $x = 0$ → Pressure-compensating
- $x = 0.5$ → Orifice-type
- $x \approx 1.0$ → Long-path labyrinth

---

## 2.3 Irrigation Scheduling

**Definition:** Irrigation scheduling is the **decision-making process** of *when* to irrigate and *how much* water to apply.

### 2.3.1 Methods of Scheduling

| Method | Principle | Tools Needed |
|--------|-----------|--------------|
| **Soil moisture-based** | Irrigate when soil water drops below critical limit | Tensiometer, gypsum block, capacitance probe, neutron probe |
| **Plant indicator-based** | Use plant stress signs (leaf wilting, canopy temp) | IR thermometer, leaf porometer |
| **Climatic (ET-based)** | Irrigate to replace evapotranspiration loss | Pan evaporimeter, weather station, Penman-Monteith |
| **Calendar/schedule-based** | Fixed interval (e.g., every 7 days) | None |

**ET-based scheduling (most scientific):**

$$\text{Net Irrigation Requirement (NIR)} = ET_c - P_{eff}$$

$$\text{ET}_c = ET_0 \times K_c$$

Where:
- $ET_0$ = reference evapotranspiration (mm/day)
- $K_c$ = crop coefficient (stage-dependent)
- $P_{eff}$ = effective rainfall
- $ET_c$ = crop ET

**Gross Irrigation Requirement:**

$$\text{GIR} = \frac{\text{NIR}}{\eta_{application}}$$

Where $\eta_{application}$ = application efficiency (0.6 – 0.9 depending on system).

---

## 2.4 Irrigation Efficiencies

### 2.4.1 Definitions (KTU-Standard Terminology)

| Term | Formula | Meaning |
|------|---------|---------|
| **Water Conveyance Efficiency (η_c)** | $\eta_c = \frac{W_f}{W_p} \times 100$ | Fraction of water at source that reaches the field |
| **Water Application Efficiency (η_a)** | $\eta_a = \frac{W_s}{W_f} \times 100$ | Fraction of water delivered to field that is stored in root zone |
| **Water Storage Efficiency (η_s)** | $\eta_s = \frac{W_s}{W_{fn}} \times 100$ | Fraction of water needed to fill root zone that is actually supplied |
| **Water Distribution Efficiency (η_d)** | $\eta_d = 100 - \left(\frac{\sigma_d}{d}\right) \times 100$ | Uniformity of water spread |
| **Overall Project Efficiency (η_p)** | $\eta_p = \eta_c \times \eta_a$ | Combined efficiency of the irrigation project |

Where:
- $W_p$ = water pumped/diverted at source
- $W_f$ = water delivered to field
- $W_s$ = water stored in root zone
- $W_{fn}$ = water needed to bring root zone to field capacity
- $\sigma_d$ = standard deviation of depths
- $d$ = average depth

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Benchmark efficiencies (ISE / FAO):**
> - Surface irrigation: 30 – 50 %
> - Sprinkler: 60 – 75 %
> - Drip: 80 – 95 %
> 
> **Drip is the gold standard** for water-saving in modern precision agriculture.

### 2.4.2 Field Capacity, PWP, and Available Water

$$\text{Available Water Capacity (AWC)} = \theta_{FC} - \theta_{PWP} \quad \text{(mm water/mm soil)}$$

$$\text{Total Available Water (TAW)} = \text{AWC} \times \text{Root depth} \quad \text{(mm)}$$

**Readily Available Water (RAW):**

$$\text{RAW} = p \cdot \text{TAW}$$

Where $p$ = depletion fraction (0.3 – 0.7 depending on crop).

Irrigation should be triggered when depletion $\geq$ RAW.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of These Concepts

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters to an Agricultural Engineer:**
> 1. **Precision irrigation** uses DO, pH, EC sensors to drive **automated fertigation** in greenhouses.
> 2. **Drone-based NDVI maps** correlate with EC and soil moisture to schedule centre-pivot irrigation.
> 3. **Aquaculture** depends entirely on continuous DO and pH monitoring — a 2 mg/L drop in DO overnight can wipe out a fish farm.
> 4. **Smart irrigation** controllers use real-time EC and turbidity to **block** irrigation when water is too saline or too dirty (avoiding emitter clogging).
> 5. **Drip irrigation** at 90 % efficiency saves ~50 % water versus flood, critical for water-stressed regions like Kerala's midlands during summer.

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Nernst Equation for pH Electrode

**Starting point:** The Nernst equation for a single electrode is:

$$E = E_0 - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{a_{red}}{a_{ox}}$$

**Step 1:** For the H⁺/H₂ half-cell, the reduction reaction is:
$$2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2$$

Here $n = 2$ (2 electrons transferred).

**Step 2:** The Nernst expression becomes:
$$E = E_0 - \frac{RT}{2F} \ln \frac{P_{H_2}}{[H^+]^2}$$

**Step 3:** Since $P_{H_2} = 1$ atm (standard hydrogen electrode, SHE), this simplifies to:
$$E = E_0 - \frac{RT}{2F} \ln \frac{1}{[H^+]^2} = E_0 + \frac{RT}{2F} \cdot 2\ln[H^+]$$

**Step 4:** Using $2.303 \cdot \log_{10} = \ln$:
$$E = E_0 + \frac{2.303 \cdot RT}{F} \log_{10}[H^+]$$

**Step 5:** Substituting $\text{pH} = -\log_{10}[H^+]$:
$$E = E_0 - \frac{2.303 \cdot RT}{F} \cdot \text{pH}$$

**Step 6:** At **T = 25 °C = 298.15 K**, with $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) and $F = 96485$ C/mol:

$$\frac{2.303 \cdot RT}{F} = \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298.15}{96485} = 0.05916 \text{ V}$$

**Step 7:** Final Nernst equation for pH electrode:
$$E = E_0 - 0.05916 \times \text{pH} \quad \text{(volts, at 25 °C)}$$

**Engineering insight:** The slope **59.16 mV/pH** is the calibration benchmark. A new probe should show 95 – 102 % of this slope; below 90 % means the probe has aged and the glass membrane must be regenerated or replaced.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Conductivity from a 4-Electrode Cell

**Step 1:** Two outer electrodes inject an AC current $I$ through the water column of length $L$ and cross-section $A$.

**Step 2:** By Ohm's law, the voltage $V$ measured between the inner two electrodes is:
$$V = I \cdot R$$

**Step 3:** The resistance $R$ of a column of electrolyte is:
$$R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$

Where $\rho$ = resistivity (Ω·cm).

**Step 4:** Substituting:
$$V = I \cdot \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \rho = \frac{V \cdot A}{I \cdot L}$$

**Step 5:** Conductivity $\kappa$ is the reciprocal of resistivity:
$$\kappa = \frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{I \cdot L}{V \cdot A}$$

**Step 6:** The **cell constant** is defined as $K = L/A$ (in cm⁻¹), so:
$$\kappa = \frac{K}{R} = K \cdot \frac{I}{V}$$

This is the working equation of the 4-electrode EC probe. The cell constant is calibrated with a standard KCl solution (e.g., 0.01 M KCl = 1.413 mS/cm at 25 °C).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Centre-Pivot Area and Wetted Fraction

**Step 1:** A centre-pivot lateral of length $R$ sweeps a full circle as it rotates.
**Step 2:** Area covered in one full revolution:
$$A_{circle} = \pi R^2$$

**Step 3:** For a square field of side $L$, the inscribed circle has radius $R = L/2$, giving:
$$A_{circle} = \frac{\pi L^2}{4} \approx 0.785 \cdot L^2$$

**Step 4:** The **wetted fraction** (efficiency of land use) is:
$$f_{wetted} = \frac{A_{circle}}{A_{field}} = \frac{\pi R^2}{L^2} = \frac{\pi}{4} \approx 78.5\%$$

**Step 5:** A **corner attachment (swing arm)** adds a span of length $r_a$ that extends the wetted area. The added area is a quarter-circle of radius $r_a$, plus the "corner triangles" swept by the swing arm.

Approximate incremental area:
$$A_{corner} \approx \frac{\pi r_a^2}{4} + 2 \cdot r_a \cdot (L - 2R)$$

**Step 6:** Total area irrigated with corner attachment:
$$A_{total} = \pi R^2 + A_{corner} \approx 0.95 \cdot L^2$$

Boosting land-use efficiency from **~78.5 % to ~95 %**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Crop Water Requirement (CWR) using Penman-Monteith (Simplified)

**Step 1:** Crop evapotranspiration is the combined loss of water from soil (E) and plant transpiration (T).

$$ET_c = ET_0 \times K_c$$

**Step 2:** The **Penman-Monteith equation** (FAO-56 form) for reference ET:

$$ET_0 = \frac{0.408 \,\Delta (R_n - G) + \gamma \,\frac{900}{T+273}\, u_2 (e_s - e_a)}{\Delta + \gamma (1 + 0.34\, u_2)}$$

Where:
- $\Delta$ = slope of saturation vapour pressure curve (kPa/°C)
- $R_n$ = net radiation (MJ/m²/day)
- $G$ = soil heat flux (MJ/m²/day)
- $\gamma$ = psychrometric constant (~0.066 kPa/°C)
- $T$ = mean daily air temperature (°C)
- $u_2$ = wind speed at 2 m height (m/s)
- $e_s - e_a$ = vapour pressure deficit (kPa)

**Step 3:** Effective rainfall (FAO method):

$$P_{eff} = \begin{cases} P \cdot \left(\frac{125 - 0.2P}{125}\right) & P \le 250\,\text{mm} \\ 125 + 0.1P & P > 250\,\text{mm} \end{cases}$$

**Step 4:** Net Irrigation Requirement:
$$\text{NIR} = ET_c - P_{eff} \quad \text{(mm/period)}$$

**Step 5:** Gross Irrigation Requirement (accounting for efficiency losses):
$$\text{GIR} = \frac{\text{NIR}}{\eta_{application}}$$

> **Worked example** (typical KTU problem):
> A tomato crop in summer has $ET_0 = 6$ mm/day, $K_c = 1.15$, rainfall negligible. Application efficiency = 80 %.
> 
> $ET_c = 6 \times 1.15 = 6.9$ mm/day
> 
> NIR = 6.9 – 0 = 6.9 mm/day
> 
> GIR = 6.9 / 0.8 = 8.625 mm/day

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Emitter Discharge Variability (CUK)

**The Christiansen Uniformity Coefficient (CUC)** quantifies how evenly water is applied:

$$CUC = 100 \times \left(1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert q_i - \bar{q} \vert}{n \cdot \bar{q}}\right)$$

Where:
- $q_i$ = discharge of $i^{th}$ emitter
- $\bar{q}$ = mean discharge
- $n$ = number of emitters sampled

**Example calculation:**

| Emitter | $q_i$ (L/h) | $\vert q_i - \bar{q} \vert$ |
|---------|-------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | 4.0 | 0.2 |
| 2 | 4.2 | 0.0 |
| 3 | 3.8 | 0.4 |
| 4 | 4.4 | 0.2 |
| 5 | 4.2 | 0.0 |

$$\bar{q} = \frac{4.0+4.2+3.8+4.4+4.2}{5} = 4.12\,\text{L/h}$$

$$\sum \vert q_i - \bar{q} \vert = 0.2+0+0.4+0.2+0 = 0.8$$

$$CUC = 100 \times \left(1 - \frac{0.8}{5 \times 4.12}\right) = 100 \times (1 - 0.0388) = 96.12\%$$

> A CUC > 90 % is **excellent**; 80 – 90 % is **acceptable**; < 80 % requires maintenance.

| Parameter | Best Sensor Principle | Range (typical) | Accuracy | Major Interference |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------|----------|---------------------|
| pH | Glass membrane + Ag/AgCl ref. | 0 – 14 | ±0.02 pH | Temperature, Na⁺ (alkaline error) |

**Reading the diagram:** The Soil-Plant-Atmosphere Continuum (SPAC) shows that water cycles through a closed loop. ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**Define pH. Explain the working of a glass-membrane pH electrode with a neat sketch.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** pH is the negative base-10 logarithm of hydrogen ion activity: $\text{pH} = -\log_{10}[H^+]$. The scale runs 0 (acidic) to 14 (alkaline), with 7 neutral.
- **Working (2 marks):** A glass-membrane electrode has a thin H⁺-sensitive glass bulb containing 0.1 M HCl and an internal Ag/AgCl reference. When dipped in a sample, H⁺ ions exchange with Na⁺ in the hydrated glass gel layer, generating a potential difference $E$ across the membrane that follows the Nernst equation: $E = E_0 - 0.0592 \cdot \text{pH}$ at 25 °C. The potential is measured against an external reference electrode and converted to pH by the meter.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**What is turbidity? How is it measured using a nephelometric turbidimeter?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Turbidity is an optical property of water caused by suspended particles (clay, silt, algae) that scatter light. Measured in **NTU (Nephelometric Turbidity Units)**.
- **Working (2 marks):** A nephelometer uses an **IR LED (860 nm)** as the light source directed at the water sample. A **silicon photodiode** placed at **90°** to the incident beam detects scattered light. The intensity of scattered light is proportional to particle concentration. A reference photodiode compensates for LED ageing. The output is converted to NTU via factory calibration with formazin standards (4000 NTU primary stock).

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Sample]**

#### (a) Explain the principle, construction, and working of a **Clark-type Dissolved Oxygen (DO) sensor** with a neat sketch. List the half-reactions. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** A Clark-type DO sensor is an **amperometric electrochemical sensor** that measures the current produced by the reduction of dissolved O₂ at a polarised gold cathode, separated from the sample by a gas-permeable membrane.

**Construction (2 marks):**
- **Au cathode** (small, central) — polarised to ~0.7 V (negative).
- **Ag/AgCl anode** (large ring) — counter-electrode in KCl electrolyte.
- **Gas-permeable membrane** (PTFE, ~25 µm thick) — allows only O₂ to diffuse.
- **Electrolyte** — saturated KCl solution.
- Body — corrosion-resistant epoxy/plastic.

**Working (2 marks):**
1. Sample water is separated from the electrode by the gas-permeable membrane.
2. O₂ diffuses through the membrane at a rate governed by **Fick's first law**:
$$J_{O_2} = -D \cdot \frac{dC}{dx}$$
3. At the Au cathode, O₂ is reduced, producing a current proportional to flux:
$$O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$$
4. The measured current (nA – µA) is directly proportional to DO concentration (mg/L) after temperature compensation.

**Half-reactions (2 marks):**
- **At cathode (Au):** $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$
- **At anode (Ag/AgCl):** $4Ag + 4Cl^- \rightarrow 4AgCl + 4e^-$

---

#### (b) Discuss the **necessity of irrigation** in modern agriculture. Compare the **working principles of centre-pivot and lateral-move sprinkler systems** with a sketch. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Necessity of Irrigation (3 marks):**
1. **Supplemental to rainfall:** Rainfall is seasonal and erratic; crops like paddy, sugarcane, and vegetables need water year-round.
2. **Higher productivity:** HYV seeds and hybrid varieties have higher transpiration needs (ETc often 6 – 8 mm/day).
3. **Soil moisture maintenance:** Prevents soil from drying below **Permanent Wilting Point (PWP)**, sustaining plant turgor.
4. **Salt leaching:** Periodic heavy irrigation flushes accumulated salts below the root zone, preventing salinisation.
5. **Multiple cropping:** Enables 2 – 3 crops per year instead of 1 rain-fed crop.
6. **Drought mitigation:** Provides insurance against monsoon failure.

**Centre-Pivot vs Lateral-Move (4 marks):**

| Feature | Centre Pivot | Lateral Move |
|---------|--------------|--------------|
| Movement | Rotates about a fixed central point | Moves linearly across the field |
| Field shape | Circle / pie-slice | Rectangular / square |
| Land-use efficiency | ~78 % (square field) | ~95 – 98 % |
| Water supply | Underground pipe from pivot | Open channel + hose or drag-shoe |
| Power source | Electric (from pivot) | Diesel generator on cart |
| Suitable terrain | Flat, large fields | Flat, large rectangular fields |
| Corner irrigation | Needs swing arm | Inherently covers corners |

**Centre-pivot sketch (already in SECTION 4.3).** The lateral hangs from mobile towers spaced ~30 m apart. The outermost tower's speed sets the rotation period (typically 12 – 36 hours for one revolution). The end gun extends the radius by 15 – 30 m.

**Lateral-move sketch (described):** The lateral is mounted on wheeled towers and moves perpendicularly across a rectangular field. Water is drawn from a **supply channel** parallel to the direction of travel via a flexible hose coupled to a "shoe" that slides along the channel.

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Sample]**

#### (a) Define **Electrical Conductivity (EC)**. With a neat circuit, explain the working of a **4-electrode conductivity probe**. How is EC related to Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)? (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** EC is the ability of an aqueous solution to conduct an electric current, which depends on the concentration of dissolved ions. The SI unit is **S/m**, but agricultural use is **dS/m** or **mS/cm**.

**4-Electrode Probe Working (4 marks):**

A 4-electrode (Kelvin) cell uses:
- **Two outer electrodes (C1, C2):** Inject a stable AC current $I$ (1 kHz, prevents polarisation) into the water.
- **Two inner electrodes (P1, P2):** Measure the resulting voltage drop $V$.

The cell constant $K = L/A$ is determined by calibration with 0.01 M KCl (κ = 1.413 mS/cm at 25 °C). The conductivity is computed as:
$$\kappa = \frac{K}{R} = K \cdot \frac{I}{V}$$

**Advantages over 2-electrode cell:**
- Eliminates electrode polarisation.
- Negligible fouling error.
- Wide range (0 – 5000 µS/cm with autoranging).

**TDS Relationship (2 marks):**

$$\text{TDS (mg/L)} \approx 0.64 \times \text{EC (dS/m)}$$

The factor **0.64** is empirical; for high-NaCl water, the factor becomes 0.55 – 0.58, while for gypsum-rich water it rises to 0.7 – 0.8. In practice, modern EC meters have a user-selectable TDS conversion factor.

---

#### (b) Explain the **components and working of a drip irrigation system**. Define **Emitter Uniformity Coefficient (CUC)** and compute it for the data given. (7 marks)

**Data:** 5 emitters with discharges (L/h): 4.0, 3.8, 4.2, 4.4, 4.1.

**Model Solution:**

**Drip Irrigation Components (4 marks):**
1. **Pump / head unit** — centrifugal pump, 1.5 – 3.0 bar pressure.
2. **Filtration system** — sand filter (primary), disc filter, screen filter (130 – 200 µm). Critical to prevent emitter clogging.
3. **Fertiliser injection unit** — venturi or fertiliser tank for chemigation/fertigation.
4. **Control valves and pressure regulators** — maintain 1.0 – 1.5 bar at the emitter.
5. **Main and sub-main lines** — PVC, Class 6.
6. **Laterals** — LDPE 12 / 16 / 20 mm, 0.5 – 1.5 mm wall thickness.
7. **Emitters / drippers** — 0.5 – 8 L/h, online or inline. Pressure-compensating (PC) or non-PC.
8. **Accessories** — air-release valve, flush valve, end cap, flow meter, pressure gauges.

**Working:** Pressurised water is filtered, fertilised, pressure-regulated, and delivered through laterals. Each emitter discharges 1 – 8 L/h at a wetting pattern centred on the plant root zone, achieving **80 – 95 % application efficiency** with minimal evaporation and deep percolation loss.

**CUC Calculation (3 marks):**

$$\bar{q} = \frac{4.0 + 3.8 + 4.2 + 4.4 + 4.1}{5} = \frac{20.5}{5} = 4.10\,\text{L/h}$$

Absolute deviations:
- $\vert 4.0 - 4.10 \vert = 0.10$
- $\vert 3.8 - 4.10 \vert = 0.30$
- $\vert 4.2 - 4.10 \vert = 0.10$
- $\vert 4.4 - 4.10 \vert = 0.30$
- $\vert 4.1 - 4.10 \vert = 0.00$

$$\sum \vert q_i - \bar{q} \vert = 0.10 + 0.30 + 0.10 + 0.30 + 0.00 = 0.80$$

$$CUC = 100 \times \left(1 - \frac{0.80}{5 \times 4.10}\right) = 100 \times (1 - 0.0390) = 96.10\%$$

> **Interpretation:** A CUC of 96 % is excellent (above 90 % benchmark), indicating uniform emitter performance and well-designed hydraulics.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost KTU Students 2 – 4 Marks Per Question:**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Always write dS/m for EC, mg/L for DO, NTU for turbidity, and mV for pH electrode potential. The board examiner deducts 0.5 marks for missing units.
> 2. **Wrong half-reactions in DO sensor:** Students often write $O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^- \rightarrow 2H_2O$ (acidic) instead of the alkaline form. Use the form that matches the KCl electrolyte — typically the alkaline version.
> 3. **Confusing CUC and DU:** DU (Distribution Uniformity) uses the **lower quartile** (25 % smallest discharges), not the mean. Many students swap these formulae.
> 4. **Centre-pivot area formula:** Use $\pi R^2$ (full circle) — students frequently use $2\pi R$ (circumference) and lose 2 marks.
> 5. **Emitter exponent confusion:** $x = 0$ = pressure-compensating (constant discharge); $x = 1$ = long-path labyrinth. Students often invert the relationship.
> 6. **Kerala-specific context:** Questions often ask "Kerala midland laterite soil" — do not answer as if for Punjab alluvial soil. The EC threshold for coconut irrigation in Kerala is **< 2.0 dS/m** (more lenient than arid regions).
> 7. **Nernst slope:** Must use **59.16 mV/pH at 25 °C**, not 0.0592 V (these are equivalent but examiners prefer the consistent unit throughout).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

### 🔹 Water Quality Parameters
- [ ] **pH** = $-\log[H^+]$; Nernst slope = **59.16 mV/pH at 25 °C**; glass membrane selective to H⁺.
- [ ] **EC** ∝ dissolved salts; range in dS/m; 4-electrode AC cell; TDS = 0.64 × EC.
- [ ] **DO** (Clark-type): Au cathode + Ag/AgCl anode + PTFE membrane; current ∝ DO; units mg/L.
- [ ] **Turbidity** in NTU; 90° IR (860 nm) nephelometry; > 1 NTU irrigable, > 5 NTU risk of emitter clogging.

### 🔹 Irrigation Systems
- [ ] **Necessity:** Supplement rain, support HYVs, leach salts, enable multiple cropping, mitigate drought.
- [ ] **Sprinkler** = 60 – 75 % efficiency; 2 – 4 bar pressure; applies water as artificial rain.
- [ ] **Centre-pivot** = fixed pivot, rotating lateral, covers a circle; ~78.5 % land use; corner arm extends to ~95 %.
- [ ] **Lateral move (linear move)** = lateral translates in a straight line; ~95 – 98 % land use; needs supply channel.
- [ ] **Drip (micro)** = highest efficiency (80 – 95 %); 1 – 1.5 bar; delivers water to root zone via emitters.

### 🔹 Drip Components (in order)
- [ ] Pump → NRV → Air valve → Sand filter → Fertigation tank → Disc filter → Screen filter → Pressure regulator → Manifold → Laterals → Emitters.

### 🔹 Irrigation Scheduling
- [ ] **Soil-based:** Tensiometer reads ≤ 20 – 40 kPa → irrigate.
- [ ] **ET-based:** $\text{NIR} = ET_0 \times K_c - P_{eff}$; $\text{GIR} = \text{NIR} / \eta_a$.
- [ ] **CUC ≥ 90 %** = excellent emitter uniformity; **TAW = AWC × root depth**; **RAW = p × TAW**.

### 🔹 Efficiencies (memorize formulas)
- [ ] $\eta_c = W_f / W_p$
- [ ] $\eta_a = W_s / W_f$
- [ ] $\eta_p = \eta_c \times \eta_a$
- [ ] $\eta_d = 100 \cdot (1 - \sigma/\bar{d})$

### 🔹 Critical Numbers to Memorize
- [ ] Nernst slope: **59.16 mV/pH**
- [ ] KCl calibration: **1.413 mS/cm** at 25 °C (0.01 M)
- [ ] TDS factor: **0.64**
- [ ] Centre-pivot land efficiency: **π/4 ≈ 0.785**
- [ ] Emitter exponent: **0** = PC, **0.5** = orifice, **1.0** = long-path
- [ ] Drip efficiency: **80 – 95 %**
- [ ] Sprinkler efficiency: **60 – 75 %**
- [ ] Surface efficiency: **30 – 50 %**

> **Final KTU Mantra:** "*Sensors measure, controllers decide, systems deliver, efficiencies save.*" Master the principles, the formulae, and the sketch — and you are set for full marks in Module 2. 🚜💧

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Greenhouse — Formal Definition

A **greenhouse** is a framed structure covered with transparent or translucent glazing material (glass, polyethylene film, polycarbonate panels, or acrylic sheets) in which environmental parameters such as **temperature, relative humidity, light intensity, and carbon dioxide concentration** can be regulated to provide optimal growing conditions for plants, irrespective of the external ambient conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** A greenhouse is an engineered micro-environment used to manipulate the **four primary growth variables** — Temperature (T), Relative Humidity (RH), Photosynthetically Active Radiation (PAR, 400–700 nm), and CO₂ concentration — to maximize crop yield, quality, and resource-use efficiency.

## 1.2 Microclimate — Formal Definition

A **microclimate** is the localized atmospheric condition prevailing in a small, specific region that differs from the surrounding macroclimate. In agriculture, it refers to the climatic conditions **immediately surrounding the plant canopy** — typically within a horizontal distance of **< 1 m to 10 m** from the leaf surface.

## 1.3 Climate Control — Formal Definition

**Climate control** in greenhouses is the closed-loop regulation of T, RH, PAR, and CO₂ using sensors, controllers, and actuators (heaters, fans, vents, foggers, shade nets, CO₂ injectors) to maintain a set-point defined by the crop phenological stage.

## 1.4 Environmental Sensors — Formal Definition

**Environmental sensors** are transducers that convert physical/chemical quantities (temperature, humidity, light flux, CO₂ partial pressure) into standardized electrical signals (mV, mA, V, or digital protocols such as Modbus, I²C, SDI-12).

## 1.5 Conceptual Analogies & Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy 1 — Greenhouse as a "Selective Sun Box":**
> Imagine a closed car parked in the sun. Short-wave solar radiation enters through the glass but cannot escape as long-wave infrared (Greenhouse Effect). The car interior warms up. A greenhouse does exactly this but **adds intelligence** — sensors monitor the inside and automatically open vents, run fans, or mist water to keep the inside climate "perfect" for plants. Without the controller, it is just a hot box; with sensors and actuators, it is a **climate factory**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy 2 — Microclimate as a "Personal Weather Bubble":**
> Stand under a big tree versus an open field. Under the tree it is cooler, damper, dimmer — you are in a **different climate**. That tiny climate around you is a microclimate. Crops experience the same — the air just above and inside the leaf canopy has its own temperature, humidity, and CO₂ profile that differs from the regional weather. Greenhouses create and protect this bubble.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy 3 — Sensors as the Greenhouse's "Five Senses":**
> A greenhouse without sensors is like a blindfolded caretaker. A **thermocouple** is its sense of touch (temperature), a **hygrometer** is its sense of feel-moisture (humidity), a **photodiode/pyranometer** is its sense of sight (light), and an **NDIR CO₂ sensor** is its sense of smell (gas detection). The controller is the brain that decides when to switch on cooling, heating, lighting, or CO₂ dosing.

## 1.6 Key Standard Metrics & Constants

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Range / Value |
|-----------|----------------|----------------------|
| Solar constant (outside atmosphere) | $G_{sc}$ | **1367 W/m²** |
| Photosynthetically Active Radiation | $PAR$ | **400 – 700 nm** |
| Optimal greenhouse temperature | $T_{opt}$ | **18 °C – 28 °C** |
| Optimal relative humidity | $RH_{opt}$ | **60 % – 80 %** |
| Ambient CO₂ concentration | $C_{atm}$ | **~ 415 ppm** |
| Enriched greenhouse CO₂ | $C_{enr}$ | **800 – 1500 ppm** |
| Stomatal conductance range | $g_s$ | **0.05 – 0.5 mol/m²/s** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion note:** $1\ \mu mol/m^2/s \approx 0.218\ W/m^2$ for PAR (using McCree/Lamers weighting). Always check units carefully in KTU numericals — examiners LOVE this trap.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Greenhouses (Structural Hierarchy)

- **Based on shape:** Quonset (arched), Gable-roof (even-span), Gothic-arch, A-frame, Sawtooth, Venlo.
- **Based on glazing:** Glass houses, Polyethylene (PE) film houses, Polycarbonate (PC) sheet houses, Acrylic houses.
- **Based on environmental control:** Low-tech (passive ventilation), Mid-tech (fan-pad cooling), High-tech (fully automated climate computer).
- **Based on use:** Production greenhouse, Research greenhouse, Shade house, Polyhouse, Screen house.

## 2.2 The Four Primary Environmental Parameters

### 2.2.1 Temperature (T)
- Measured in °C or K.
- Too high → photorespiration, flower abortion, fruit cracking.
- Too low → chilling injury, slowed enzymatic activity.
- **Control devices:** heaters (tubular, forced-air gas, hot water), ventilators (roof + side), exhaust fans, thermal screens, evaporative cooling pads.

### 2.2.2 Relative Humidity (RH)
- Ratio of actual water-vapor pressure ($e_a$) to saturation vapor pressure ($e_s$): $RH = \dfrac{e_a}{e_s} \times 100\%$.
- High RH → fungal diseases (Botrytis, powdery mildew), reduced transpiration.
- Low RH → stomatal closure, calcium deficiency (blossom-end rot).
- **Control devices:** foggers/misters (raise RH), exhaust fans + heating (lower RH), horizontal airflow (HAF) fans.

### 2.2.3 Light (PAR)
- Measured as **Photosynthetic Photon Flux Density (PPFD)** in $\mu mol/m^2/s$, or irradiance in $W/m^2$.
- Light saturation point: tomato ~ 700 $\mu mol/m^2/s$; lettuce ~ 400 $\mu mol/m^2/s$.
- **Control devices:** shade nets, retractable screens, supplemental lighting (HPS, LED), photoperiodic lighting.

### 2.2.4 CO₂ Concentration
- Outside: ~415 ppm; inside well-ventilated: ~400 ppm; enriched: 800–1500 ppm.
- Compensation point: ~50–100 ppm; saturation point: ~1000 ppm for C₃ plants.
- **Control devices:** CO₂ burners (natural gas), liquid CO₂ dosing, pure CO₂ cylinders with solenoid valves.

## 2.3 Sensor Principles — Operational Logic

### 2.3.1 Hair Hygrometer (Humidity Sensor)
- **Principle:** Human/animal hair (especially blonde, degreased human hair) changes length with relative humidity due to adsorption of water molecules between keratin fibrils.
- **Mechanism:** Hygroscopic swelling → mechanical displacement → lever amplification → pointer on dial OR LVDT/encoder output.
- **Typical range:** 0 % – 100 % RH; accuracy ±3 % RH; response time 30 s – 2 min.
- **Why it works:** Keratin contains amino acids (-COOH, -NH₂) that form hydrogen bonds with water vapor; more water → more bonds → hair lengthens.

### 2.3.2 Dry and Wet Bulb Hygrometer (Psychrometer)
- **Principle:** Evaporative cooling of a wet-bulb thermometer.
- Two identical thermometers; one bulb is wrapped in muslin/cotton wick kept wet (wet bulb), the other is exposed to air (dry bulb).
- Evaporation from the wet bulb absorbs latent heat → wet-bulb temperature ($T_w$) drops below dry-bulb ($T_d$).
- The depression $T_d - T_w$ is a function of RH.

### 2.3.3 Photodiode (Light Sensor)
- **Principle:** Photovoltaic effect — incident photons with $E > E_g$ generate electron-hole pairs in a p-n junction, producing a photocurrent.
- Output: current proportional to incident irradiance (typically silicon photodiodes for PAR range).
- Spectral response typically 400 – 1100 nm; with filter, can be tuned to PAR.

### 2.3.4 Pyranometer (Solar Radiation Sensor)
- **Principle:** Thermopile-based sensor that measures **broadband solar irradiance** (300 – 2800 nm) on a horizontal surface.
- Black-and-white or all-black absorbing surface heats up; the temperature difference between hot and cold junctions of a thermopile produces a millivolt signal proportional to irradiance.
- Output: $\mu V$ per $W/m^2$; ISO 9060 classes: Class A (secondary standard), Class B, Class C.

### 2.3.5 NDIR (Non-Dispersive Infrared) CO₂ Sensor
- **Principle:** Selective absorption of infrared radiation by CO₂ molecules at specific wavelengths (~ 4.26 μm — fundamental asymmetric stretch vibration band).
- An IR source emits broadband IR → passes through measurement chamber containing air sample → falls on IR detector.
- CO₂ absorbs IR at 4.26 μm → intensity reduction follows the **Beer-Lambert Law**.
- A reference detector (or optical filter at non-absorbing wavelength) compensates for source aging, dust, and optical drift.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these formulas appear repeatedly in KTU End Semester Examinations.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions | Units |
|---|---------|---------|----------------------|-------|
| 1 | Relative Humidity | $RH = \dfrac{e_a}{e_s(T)} \times 100$ | $e_a$ actual vapor pressure; $e_s(T)$ saturation vapor pressure at T | % |
| 2 | Saturation Vapor Pressure (Tetens) | $e_s(T) = 0.6108 \cdot e^{\frac{17.27\,T}{T + 237.3}}$ | $T$ in °C | kPa |
| 3 | Actual Vapor Pressure from Wet-Dry Bulb | $e_a = e_s(T_w) - \gamma \cdot P \cdot (T_d - T_w)$ | $\gamma$ psychrometric constant (≈ 0.000665 kPa/°C at standard atm); P pressure | kPa |
| 4 | Psychrometric Constant | $\gamma = \dfrac{c_p \cdot P}{\lambda \cdot \varepsilon}$ | $c_p$ specific heat of dry air (1.013 kJ/kg·K); $\lambda$ latent heat of vaporization (2450 kJ/kg at 20°C); $\varepsilon$ ratio of gas constants (0.622) | kPa/°C |
| 5 | Dew Point Temperature | $T_d = \dfrac{237.3 \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{e_a}{0.6108}\right)}{17.3 - \ln\left(\dfrac{e_a}{0.6108}\right)}$ | from Magnus formula inversion | °C |
| 6 | Beer-Lambert Law (NDIR) | $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha \cdot c \cdot L}$ | $I$ transmitted; $I_0$ incident; $\alpha$ absorption coefficient; $c$ concentration; $L$ path length | varies |
| 7 | NDIR Output | $V_{out} = K \cdot (1 - e^{-\alpha c L})$ | $K$ calibration constant | V, ppm |
| 8 | PPFD from Irradiance | $PPFD \approx 4.57 \cdot E_{PAR}$ | $E_{PAR}$ in W/m²; result in $\mu mol/m^2/s$ | $\mu mol/m^2/s$ |
| 9 | Solar Declination | $\delta = 23.45 \cdot \sin\left(\dfrac{360}{365}(284 + n)\right)$ | $n$ day of year | ° |
| 10 | Greenhouse Heat Loss (Conductive) | $Q_c = U \cdot A \cdot (T_i - T_o)$ | $U$ overall heat transfer coefficient; $A$ surface area | W |
| 11 | Ventilation Rate | $\dot{V} = \dfrac{Q_{sensible}}{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot (T_i - T_o)}$ | $\rho$ air density (1.2 kg/m³) | m³/s |
| 12 | Daily Light Integral | $DLI = \int_{sunrise}^{sunset} PPFD(t) \, dt$ | time-integrated PPFD | mol/m²/day |
| 13 | Hair Hygrometer Sensitivity | $\dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} = k \cdot \Delta RH$ | $k$ ≈ 1.5 × 10⁻⁴ per %RH | dimensionless |
| 14 | Quantum Yield of Photosynthesis | $\Phi = \dfrac{\mu mol\ CO_2\ fixed}{\mu mol\ photons\ absorbed}$ | dimensionless ratio | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When calculating humidity, ALWAYS use Tetens/Magnus formula for $e_s$ — never assume saturation tables are given. KTU numericals typically expect you to **derive** $e_a$ step-by-step.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Subsystem | Industry / Application |
|-----------|------------------------|
| Precision greenhouses | Tomato, cucumber, capsicum production (Netherlands, Israel, India) |
| Floriculture | Rose, gerbera cut-flower houses (Kenya, Ecuador, India) |
| Plant factories | Vertical farms with LED-only lighting (Japan, Singapore) |
| Research phytotrons | Controlled-environment agriculture (CERES, Jülich) |
| Tissue culture labs | Acclimatization chambers with strict RH 70–90 % |
| Seedling nurseries | Low tunnels + polyhouse hybrid for vegetable seedlings |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of RH from Wet-and-Dry Bulb Hygrometer Readings

**Given:** Dry-bulb temperature $T_d$ = 30 °C, Wet-bulb temperature $T_w$ = 22 °C, Atmospheric pressure $P$ = 101.325 kPa.

**Find:** Relative Humidity (RH).

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-step derivation** — every algebraic transition is explicitly shown.

**Step 1:** Write the psychrometric equation (with simplified psychrometric constant for normal atmospheric pressure):

$$e_a = e_s(T_w) - \gamma \cdot (T_d - T_w)$$

where $\gamma \approx 0.000665\ kPa/°C$ at $P = 101.325$ kPa (for air).

**Step 2:** Compute $e_s(T_w)$ using Tetens equation with $T_w = 22\ °C$:

$$e_s(T_w) = 0.6108 \cdot e^{\frac{17.27 \cdot 22}{22 + 237.3}}$$

$$\frac{17.27 \cdot 22}{259.3} = \frac{379.94}{259.3} = 1.4653$$

$$e^{1.4653} = 4.329$$

$$e_s(T_w) = 0.6108 \times 4.329 = 2.645\ kPa$$

**Step 3:** Compute the depression term:

$$\gamma \cdot (T_d - T_w) = 0.000665 \times (30 - 22) = 0.000665 \times 8 = 0.00532\ kPa$$

**Step 4:** Compute actual vapor pressure $e_a$:

$$e_a = 2.645 - 0.00532 = 2.6397\ kPa$$

**Step 5:** Compute $e_s(T_d)$ using Tetens equation with $T_d = 30\ °C$:

$$e_s(T_d) = 0.6108 \cdot e^{\frac{17.27 \cdot 30}{30 + 237.3}}$$

$$\frac{17.27 \cdot 30}{267.3} = \frac{518.1}{267.3} = 1.9383$$

$$e^{1.9383} = 6.948$$

$$e_s(T_d) = 0.6108 \times 6.948 = 4.244\ kPa$$

**Step 6:** Compute Relative Humidity:

$$RH = \frac{e_a}{e_s(T_d)} \times 100 = \frac{2.6397}{4.244} \times 100$$

$$RH = 62.20\ \%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analytical interpretation:** A wet-bulb depression of 8 °C at 30 °C dry-bulb corresponds to **moderately dry air** (~62 %). Comfortable for crops like tomato but on the lower side for cucumber (which prefers 70–80 %).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Psychrometric Constant $\gamma$

The psychrometric constant governs the rate at which wet-bulb temperature falls as water evaporates.

**Step 1:** Define latent heat of vaporization $\lambda$ at temperature $T$:

$$\lambda(T) = 2501 - 2.361 \cdot T\ \ [kJ/kg]$$

At $T = 20\ °C$: $\lambda = 2501 - 2.361 \times 20 = 2453.78\ kJ/kg$.

**Step 2:** Define specific heat of moist air at constant pressure:

$$c_p \approx 1.013\ kJ/(kg \cdot K)\ \text{(dry air approximation)}$$

**Step 3:** Define ratio of molecular weight of water vapor to dry air:

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{M_w}{M_a} = \dfrac{18.015}{28.964} = 0.622$$

**Step 4:** Write the energy balance on the wet bulb. Latent heat absorbed by evaporation equals sensible heat lost by air cooling:

$$\lambda \cdot \dot{m}_w = c_p \cdot \rho_a \cdot (T_d - T_w)$$

The mass of water evaporated per unit mass of dry air is $\dot{m}_w = \dfrac{\varepsilon \cdot (e_s(T_w) - e_a)}{P - e_s(T_w)}$.

**Step 5:** Substitute and simplify (assuming $P \gg e_s$, so $P - e_s \approx P$):

$$\gamma = \dfrac{c_p \cdot P}{\lambda \cdot \varepsilon}$$

**Step 6:** Plug numerical values:

$$\gamma = \dfrac{1.013 \times 101.325}{2453.78 \times 0.622}$$

$$\gamma = \dfrac{102.62}{1526.25}$$

$$\gamma = 0.06723\ kPa/°C$$

Wait — this is the **thermodynamic** psychrometric constant. The **actual** psychrometric constant used in the wet-bulb equation depends on the **instrument's ventilation speed**. At standard sling psychrometer velocity (~5 m/s):

$$\gamma_{sling} \approx 0.000665\ kPa/°C$$

At low ventilation (natural draft), $\gamma$ is smaller (~0.0004). KTU numericals typically use $\gamma = 0.000665\ kPa/°C$ — always **state your assumption**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of NDIR CO₂ Sensor Response

**Step 1:** A broadband IR source (often a tungsten filament or MEMS emitter) emits IR radiation with intensity $I_0(\lambda)$ across the spectrum.

**Step 2:** The IR beam passes through the sample gas cell of path length $L$ (typically 1–50 mm) containing CO₂ at concentration $c$ (ppm).

**Step 3:** Apply the **Beer-Lambert Law** for monochromatic radiation at the CO₂ absorption band ($\lambda \approx 4.26\ \mu m$):

$$I(\lambda) = I_0(\lambda) \cdot e^{-\alpha(\lambda) \cdot c \cdot L}$$

where $\alpha(\lambda)$ is the wavelength-dependent absorption coefficient of CO₂.

**Step 4:** The detector (typically a thermopile or pyroelectric with an optical bandpass filter at 4.26 $\mu m$) measures the transmitted intensity $I$.

**Step 5:** The reference detector at a non-absorbing wavelength (e.g., 3.9 $\mu m$ or 4.0 $\mu m$ where CO₂ does not absorb) measures $I_{ref}$.

**Step 6:** The ratio eliminates source-intensity fluctuations:

$$R = \dfrac{I}{I_{ref}} = e^{-\alpha \cdot c \cdot L}$$

**Step 7:** Take natural logarithm and solve for $c$:

$$c = -\dfrac{1}{\alpha \cdot L} \ln(R)$$

**Step 8:** With calibration, this is linearized for small $c \cdot L$ (single-pass) using $e^{-x} \approx 1 - x$:

$$c \approx \dfrac{1 - R}{\alpha \cdot L}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why NDIR is preferred over chemical sensors for CO₂:** It is non-consuming (no chemical reaction, no electrolyte to replace), highly selective (only CO₂ absorbs at 4.26 $\mu m$), and has a lifetime of > 10 years. KTU examiners expect this comparison.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Greenhouse Daily Light Integral (DLI)

**Given:** A polyhouse in summer receives an average PPFD of 600 $\mu mol/m^2/s$ from sunrise (06:00) to sunset (18:30).

**Step 1:** Convert total daylight hours to seconds:

$$t = 12.5\ h \times 3600\ s/h = 45{,}000\ s$$

**Step 2:** Apply DLI definition:

$$DLI = \int_{sunrise}^{sunset} PPFD(t) \, dt \approx PPFD_{avg} \times t$$

$$DLI = 600 \times 45{,}000 = 27{,}000{,}000\ \mu mol/m^2$$

**Step 3:** Convert to mol/m²/day:

$$DLI = \dfrac{27 \times 10^6}{10^6} = 27\ mol/m^2/day$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crop DLI requirements (high-yield KTU fact):** Tomato needs ~20–30 mol/m²/day; cucumber 15–25; lettuce 12–17; rose 18–25. Below the requirement → poor growth; far above → photoinhibition.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Comparison

| Sensor | Output Signal | Range | Accuracy | Response Time | Maintenance |
|--------|---------------|-------|----------|---------------|-------------|
| Hair hygrometer | Mechanical displacement / mV | 0–100 % RH | ±3 % RH | 30 s – 2 min | Wick wetting; periodic calibration |
| Wet-dry bulb psychrometer | Two T readings → derived RH | 10–100 % RH | ±2 % RH | 1–5 min | Distilled water; wick cleaning |
| Capacitive RH sensor | Capacitance (pF) / V | 0–100 % RH | ±2 % RH | 5–30 s | Low; protect from condensation |
| Photodiode (PAR) | Photocurrent ($\mu A$) | 0–2500 $\mu mol/m^2/s$ | ±5 % | < 1 $\mu s$ | Cosine correction; window cleaning |
| Pyranometer (ISO 9060 Class B) | Thermopile $\mu V$ per $W/m^2$ | 0–2000 $W/m^2$ | ±5 % daily | ~ 5–30 s | Dome cleaning; desiccant check |
| NDIR CO₂ sensor | Voltage / digital (UART, I²C) | 0–5000 ppm | ±30 ppm + 3 % | 10–30 s | Annual calibration; dust filter |
| Thermocouple (Type T) | mV | -200 to +400 °C | ±0.5 °C | < 1 s | Cold-junction compensation |
| PT100 RTD | Resistance $\Omega$ | -50 to +400 °C | ±0.1 °C | 3–5 s | Lead-wire compensation |

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical — Greenhouse Ventilation Requirement

**Problem:** A greenhouse has internal sensible heat load $Q_s = 45\ kW$, inside temperature $T_i = 30\ °C$, outside $T_o = 35\ °C$. Find the required ventilation airflow.

**Step 1:** Apply the sensible heat balance:

$$\dot{V} = \dfrac{Q_s}{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot (T_o - T_i)}$$

**Step 2:** Plug values: $\rho = 1.2\ kg/m^3$, $c_p = 1.013\ kJ/(kg \cdot K)$, $\Delta T = 35 - 30 = 5\ °C$:

$$\dot{V} = \dfrac{45}{1.2 \times 1.013 \times 5}$$

$$\dot{V} = \dfrac{45}{6.078}$$

$$\dot{V} = 7.404\ m^3/s$$

**Step 3:** Convert to common ventilation units:

$$\dot{V} = 7.404 \times 3600 = 26{,}656\ m^3/h$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design check:** For a 1000 m² greenhouse with 4 m height, this is **2.66 air changes per hour** — moderate. KTU expects you to comment on whether this is sufficient for the crop.
