# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part A questions test direct recall, definition, or a small sketch. Each carries **3 marks**. Answers should be 3–5 sentences or one labeled diagram.

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the working principle of a hair hygrometer. List its advantages and limitations.**

**Model Answer:**
A hair hygrometer measures relative humidity using the principle of **hygroscopic elongation of human/animal hair**. When RH increases, water molecules are adsorbed between keratin fibrils of the hair, causing it to lengthen by ~1.5 × 10⁻⁴ per % RH. This small displacement is mechanically amplified via a lever linkage and indicated on a calibrated dial (or converted to an electrical signal via LVDT).

**Advantages:** (i) Simple, cheap, no power required for passive type; (ii) reasonably accurate (±3 % RH) for greenhouse use; (iii) long-term stable when kept clean.

**Limitations:** (i) Slow response (30 s to several minutes); (ii) requires periodic recalibration as hair ages; (iii) affected by dust and chemical vapors; (iv) hysteresis error around ±2 % RH; (v) not suitable for very low temperatures (< 0 °C).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define microclimate. Why is microclimate monitoring important in greenhouse and field agriculture?**

**Model Answer:**
A **microclimate** is the climate of a small, specific area that differs from the surrounding regional (macro) climate. It is characterized by localized values of temperature, humidity, wind, radiation, and gas concentration, typically measured within **1 cm to 10 m** of the surface of interest (soil, leaf, animal).

**Importance:** (i) Plants respond to the **air immediately surrounding their leaves**, not the weather-station reading several kilometers away. (ii) Inside a crop canopy, T can be 2–5 °C higher than ambient, RH 10–20 % higher, CO₂ 50–100 ppm lower than above the canopy due to photosynthesis. (iii) Microclimate monitoring enables **precision irrigation, fertigation, and disease prediction** (e.g., leaf wetness duration for fungal outbreaks). (iv) In greenhouses, spatial variability (corners vs. center) can cause yield differences of 15–25 %.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part B carries **14 marks**. KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Module Internal Choice allows students to attempt **either Question A or Question B** from each module. Each question typically has **two sub-parts: (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### ✦ QUESTION A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Describe the four major environmental parameters that need to be controlled in a greenhouse. For each parameter, state its optimal range, the sensor used, and one control device. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of an **NDIR CO₂ sensor**. Derive the relationship between CO₂ concentration and output voltage using the Beer-Lambert Law. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a)

The four major environmental parameters in a greenhouse are:

**(1) Temperature (T)**
- Optimal range: **18 – 28 °C** depending on crop phenological stage.
- Sensor: **Thermocouple (Type T) or PT100 RTD** for air temperature; **infrared thermometer** for leaf temperature.
- Control device: **Forced-air gas heater / hot water pipe** (heating); **roof vent + exhaust fan / fan-pad cooling system** (cooling).

**(2) Relative Humidity (RH)**
- Optimal range: **60 – 80 %** for most crops.
- Sensor: **Hair hygrometer** (mechanical), **capacitive polymer RH sensor** (electronic), or **wet-dry bulb psychrometer** (reference standard).
- Control device: **Misting/fogging system** (raise RH); **exhaust fan + heating** (lower RH via warm-air venting).

**(3) Light (PAR)**
- Optimal range: **PPFD 200 – 800 $\mu mol/m^2/s$** depending on crop; DLI 12–30 mol/m²/day.
- Sensor: **Quantum sensor** (PAR-specific photodiode with filter) or **pyranometer** (broadband).
- Control device: **Shade net / retractable thermal screen** (reduce); **HPS or LED supplemental lighting** (increase).

**(4) Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)**
- Optimal range: **400 – 1500 ppm** (ambient to enriched).
- Sensor: **NDIR CO₂ sensor** (preferred) or electrochemical CO₂ sensor.
- Control device: **Liquid CO₂ doser / pure CO₂ cylinder with solenoid** (enrichment); **roof vents** (deplete).

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b)

**Construction (Diagram Required):**
An NDIR sensor consists of:
1. An **infrared source** — usually a tungsten filament lamp or MEMS-based pulsed emitter.
2. A **sample gas chamber** — a tube of path length $L$ (typically 1–50 mm) with IR-transparent windows (typically calcium fluoride or sapphire).
3. An **optical bandpass filter** tuned to **4.26 $\mu m$** — the fundamental absorption wavelength of CO₂.
4. A **thermal IR detector** — thermopile or pyroelectric sensor.
5. A **reference channel** — same source, but filter at a non-absorbing wavelength (3.9 $\mu m$) to compensate for lamp drift.
6. **Signal conditioning electronics**, ADC, and microcontroller with UART/I²C output.

**Working Principle:**
IR radiation from the source passes through the sample chamber. CO₂ molecules absorb IR photons at 4.26 $\mu m$ via excitation of their asymmetric stretch vibrational mode. The transmitted intensity $I$ at 4.26 $\mu m$ is measured; the reference intensity $I_{ref}$ is measured at 3.9 $\mu m$. The ratio $I/I_{ref}$ is processed.

**Derivation using Beer-Lambert Law:**

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha \cdot c \cdot L}$$

At the reference wavelength (no absorption), $I_{ref} = I_0$.

Therefore the measured ratio:

$$\dfrac{I}{I_{ref}} = e^{-\alpha \cdot c \cdot L}$$

Taking natural log:

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{I}{I_{ref}}\right) = -\alpha \cdot c \cdot L$$

Solving for concentration $c$:

$$c = -\dfrac{1}{\alpha \cdot L} \ln\left(\dfrac{I}{I_{ref}}\right)$$

For small $\alpha c L$ (single-pass, low concentration), linearizing $e^{-x} \approx 1 - x$:

$$c \approx \dfrac{1}{\alpha L}\left(1 - \dfrac{I}{I_{ref}}\right)$$

The output voltage $V_{out}$ is proportional to $(1 - I/I_{ref})$:

$$\boxed{V_{out} = K \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{I}{I_{ref}}\right) \approx K \cdot \alpha \cdot c \cdot L}$$

where $K$ is a calibration constant determined against reference gas mixtures.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfalls in NDIR Question:**
> - **Mistake 1:** Writing Beer-Lambert as $I = I_0 e^{-kc}$ without the path length $L$. Always include $L$ — single-pass path length is a critical variable. (–1 mark)
> - **Mistake 2:** Forgetting to mention the **reference channel** — without it, the sensor drifts over months. Examiners expect this term to be in the block diagram. (–2 marks)
> - **Mistake 3:** Not stating the **specific absorption wavelength (4.26 $\mu m$)** — this is what makes the sensor "non-dispersive and selective." (–1 mark)
> - **Mistake 4:** Confusing NDIR with electrochemical CO₂ sensor — NDIR is non-consuming, electrochemical has electrolyte that depletes. (–1 mark)

---

### ✦ QUESTION B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **dry-and-wet bulb hygrometer (psychrometer)**. Derive the psychrometric equation used to compute RH from the two temperatures. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the working principle of a **pyranometer**. Compare it with a photodiode-based quantum (PAR) sensor. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a)

**Construction:**
The psychrometer consists of two identical mercury-in-glass (or thermistor) thermometers mounted side-by-side on a wooden or plastic frame.
- The **dry-bulb thermometer** is exposed directly to ambient air.
- The **wet-bulb thermometer** has its bulb wrapped in a muslin/cotton wick, the lower end of which dips into a small distilled-water reservoir.
- A **sling or aspirated fan** moves air over both bulbs at 3–5 m/s (standard ventilation rate).

**Working Principle:**
Water evaporates from the wet wick into the surrounding unsaturated air. The latent heat required for evaporation (≈ 2450 kJ/kg at 20 °C) is drawn from the wet bulb, lowering its temperature. The wet-bulb temperature $T_w$ stabilizes when the rate of heat supply from the air equals the rate of heat loss by evaporation. The temperature difference $T_d - T_w$ (wet-bulb depression) is a measure of air dryness — larger depression → drier air.

**Derivation of Psychrometric Equation:**
Consider a parcel of dry air passing over the wet bulb. Let $e_s(T_w)$ be the saturation vapor pressure at the wet-bulb surface and $e_a$ the ambient vapor pressure.

The water vapor evaporated per unit time (mass flux) is proportional to $(e_s - e_a)$:

$$\dot{m}_w \propto (e_s(T_w) - e_a)$$

The latent heat absorbed equals:

$$Q_{latent} = \lambda \cdot \dot{m}_w = \lambda \cdot A \cdot h_m \cdot (e_s(T_w) - e_a) / P$$

where $h_m$ is the mass-transfer coefficient and $A$ is wick surface area.

The sensible heat supplied by the air cooling from $T_d$ to $T_w$ is:

$$Q_{sensible} = c_p \cdot \rho_a \cdot A \cdot h_s \cdot (T_d - T_w)$$

where $h_s$ is the heat-transfer coefficient.

At steady state, $Q_{latent} = Q_{sensible}$:

$$\lambda \cdot h_m \cdot (e_s(T_w) - e_a) = c_p \cdot \rho_a \cdot P \cdot h_s \cdot (T_d - T_w)$$

Forced convection: $h_s / h_m \approx \rho_a \cdot c_p \cdot P$ (Lewis relation). Combining:

$$e_a = e_s(T_w) - \gamma \cdot (T_d - T_w)$$

where $\gamma = \dfrac{c_p \cdot P}{\lambda \cdot \varepsilon} \approx 0.000665\ kPa/°C$ at $P = 101.325\ kPa$.

Finally:

$$\boxed{RH = \dfrac{e_a}{e_s(T_d)} \times 100\ \%}$$

with both $e_s$ values computed via the Tetens formula:

$$e_s(T) = 0.6108 \cdot e^{\frac{17.27 \cdot T}{T + 237.3}}$$

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b)

**Pyranometer — Construction & Working:**
A pyranometer is a thermopile-based radiometer that measures **broadband hemispherical solar irradiance** (300 – 2800 nm) on a horizontal surface. Key components:

1. **Glass dome** — double-dome construction minimizes convection losses and protects from wind/rain.
2. **Black absorber surface** — receives solar radiation and heats up.
3. **Thermopile** — series of thermocouples whose hot junctions are in contact with the absorber and cold junctions with the shaded housing. The temperature difference $\Delta T$ generates a millivolt signal.
4. **White/reflective shield** — around the absorber to reduce lateral heat loss.

The millivolt output is calibrated against a reference pyrheliometer (WMO standard) to give sensitivity typically **5 – 20 $\mu V$ per $W/m^2$**.

**Working Principle:**
Solar radiation $\rightarrow$ absorbed by black surface $\rightarrow$ heating $\rightarrow$ temperature difference $\rightarrow$ thermoelectric EMF:

$$V_{out} = N \cdot S \cdot \Delta T$$

where $N$ = number of thermocouple junctions, $S$ = Seebeck coefficient (~ 40 $\mu V/°C$ for chromel-constantan). The output voltage is directly proportional to the irradiance $E$ (W/m²).

**Pyranometer vs. Photodiode Quantum Sensor:**

| Feature | Pyranometer | Quantum (PAR) Sensor |
|---------|-------------|----------------------|
| Spectral range | 300 – 2800 nm (broadband) | 400 – 700 nm (PAR only) |
| Sensor element | Thermopile | Silicon photodiode + optical filter |
| Output | $\mu V$ per $W/m^2$ | $\mu A$ or V per $\mu mol/m^2/s$ |
| Response time | 5 – 30 s (slow, thermal) | < 1 $\mu s$ (fast) |
| Accuracy | ±2 % (Class A) to ±5 % (Class C) | ±5 % (after cosine correction) |
| Best use | Total solar energy / energy balance | Photosynthesis studies / DLI |
| Cost | High (₹ 30,000 – 1,50,000) | Moderate (₹ 5,000 – 25,000) |
| Sensitivity to wavelength | Equal across broadband | Weighted to plant response |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pyranometer vs. Photodiode Question:**
> - **Mistake 1:** Stating pyranometer measures "light" without specifying **broadband solar irradiance in W/m²**. Examiners want units AND spectrum. (–1 mark)
> - **Mistake 2:** Not mentioning that a **quantum sensor uses an optical filter** to restrict response to 400–700 nm. Without the filter, a silicon photodiode responds well into the IR — useless for PAR. (–2 marks)
> - **Mistake 3:** Forgetting the **Seebeck effect** as the working principle of thermopile — students often write "heat → voltage" without naming it. (–1 mark)
> - **Mistake 4:** Conflating "irradiance" (W/m²) with "photon flux" ($\mu mol/m^2/s$) — they are NOT the same dimension. (–2 marks)
> - **Mistake 5:** In psychrometer derivation, omitting the **psychrometric constant** $\gamma$ and writing only $e_a = e_s(T_w)$. Always include the $\gamma(T_d - T_w)$ term. (–3 marks)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-revision checklist — read this on the morning of the exam.**

### 🔑 Greenhouse Basics
- [ ] Greenhouse = framed + glazed structure for controlled-environment agriculture.
- [ ] Four controlled parameters: **T, RH, PAR, CO₂** (KTU's "magic four").
- [ ] Microclimate = localized climate within < 10 m of plant canopy.
- [ ] DLI = mol photons / m² / day; tomato needs 20–30 mol/m²/day.

### 🔑 Temperature Control
- [ ] Sensors: PT100 (precision), Type T thermocouple (fast), infrared (leaf).
- [ ] Heating: hot water pipes, forced-air gas heaters.
- [ ] Cooling: roof vents, exhaust fans, fan-pad evaporative cooling, shade nets.

### 🔑 Humidity Control & Sensors
- [ ] **Hair hygrometer** = hygroscopic hair elongation (mechanical, ±3 % RH).
- [ ] **Wet-dry bulb psychrometer** = evaporative cooling; uses $\gamma = 0.000665\ kPa/°C$.
- [ ] **Tetens equation**: $e_s(T) = 0.6108 \cdot e^{\frac{17.27 T}{T + 237.3}}$ — **memorize this** for numericals.
- [ ] Capacitive polymer sensors are modern electronic replacements for hair hygrometers.

### 🔑 Light Sensors
- [ ] **Photodiode** = photovoltaic current; used for fast PAR sensing.
- [ ] **Quantum sensor** = photodiode + 400–700 nm filter → measures PPFD.
- [ ] **Pyranometer** = thermopile + black absorber → broadband W/m².
- [ ] Class A pyranometer is the **WMO reference**; accuracy ±2 %.

### 🔑 CO₂ Sensors
- [ ] **NDIR sensor** = selective IR absorption at 4.26 $\mu m$.
- [ ] **Beer-Lambert Law**: $I = I_0 e^{-\alpha c L}$ — must include path length $L$.
- [ ] Reference channel at 3.9 $\mu m$ prevents drift.
- [ ] NDIR is **non-consuming**; preferred over electrochemical for long-term greenhouse use.

### 🔑 Microclimate Monitoring
- [ ] Multi-point sensing at **three canopy heights** captures vertical T, RH, CO₂ gradients.
- [ ] CO₂ inside dense canopy can drop to **< 200 ppm** at midday → limits photosynthesis.
- [ ] Leaf wetness duration is a key microclimate parameter for **disease forecasting**.
- [ ] IoT-based wireless sensor networks enable **precision agriculture** decision-making.

### 🔑 Common Pitfalls in KTU Exams
- [ ] Units: convert $\mu mol/m^2/s$ ↔ $W/m^2$ carefully ($1\ \mu mol \approx 0.218\ W$ for PAR).
- [ ] Tetens equation — memorize or derive; KTU will NOT provide it in the question paper for 14-mark numericals.
- [ ] Always state **assumptions** (e.g., $\gamma = 0.000665\ kPa/°C$, $P = 101.325\ kPa$).
- [ ] Diagrams must be **labeled** with all components — unlabelled diagrams fetch 0–1 mark.
- [ ] Numerical answers must show **all working steps** — partial marking depends on it.

### 🔑 KTU Exam Day Strategy
- [ ] For 14-mark questions, allocate **~ 25 minutes per sub-part**.
- [ ] For 3-mark questions, allocate **~ 5 minutes each**.
- [ ] Always attempt the **internal choice** option you are most confident about.
- [ ] Draw **block diagrams** (not physical sketches) — KTU rewards clean flowcharts.
- [ ] End every numerical with a **boxed answer** and a **1-line interpretation** (e.g., "62 % RH — moderately dry, suitable for tomato").

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Plant Growth Monitoring

**Definition:** Plant growth monitoring refers to the systematic, quantitative measurement of physical, physiological, and biochemical parameters of crops using contact and non-contact sensors to evaluate biomass accumulation, health status, water relations, and metabolic activity in real time.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a plant as a "biological factory." Just as a factory manager installs meters on assembly lines to track output, energy use, and waste, an agricultural engineer installs sensors on plants to measure "production output" (leaf area, length), "energy input" (light/radiation), "water consumption" (evapotranspiration), and "machine activity" (respiration/photosynthesis). Without these meters, the farmer is essentially running the factory blind.

### 1.1.1 Leaf Area Measurement
- **Definition:** Leaf Area (LA) is the one-sided projected surface area of a leaf, expressed in **cm²** or **m²**. It is the primary driver of light interception, transpiration, and photosynthetic carbon gain.
- **Standard metric:** **Leaf Area Index (LAI)** = Total one-sided leaf area per unit ground area (dimensionless). Typical crops have LAI between **1 and 6**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LAI Thresholding for KTU:** Remember the critical LAI value of **3** – above this, the canopy is "closed" and 95% of incoming PAR (Photosynthetically Active Radiation) is intercepted.

### 1.1.2 Leaf Length / Plant Height Measurement
- **Definition:** Linear dimension of the plant (stem height, leaf length, root depth) typically measured in **cm** or **m**.
- **Sensing methods:** Ultrasonic time-of-flight sensors, LiDAR, photogrammetry, and structured-light 3D cameras.

### 1.1.3 Evapotranspiration (ET)
- **Definition:** The combined process by which water is transferred from the soil and plant surfaces to the atmosphere via **evaporation** (E) and **transpiration** (T). Standard reference: FAO-56 **Penman-Monteith equation** yields **ET₀ (Reference ET)** in **mm/day**.
- **Physical constant:** Latent heat of vaporization of water, **λ = 2.45 MJ/kg** at 20°C.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Reference crop ET (ET₀) is calculated using meteorological data (solar radiation, wind, humidity, temperature). Crop ET is then derived as **ETc = Kc × ET₀**, where Kc is the crop coefficient.

### 1.1.4 Leaf Wetness Measurement
- **Definition:** Detection of the presence and duration of a water film (dew, rain, guttation) on leaf surfaces, critical for disease forecasting (e.g., late blight in potato, apple scab).
- **Common Sensor:** Flat-plate capacitive sensor (dielectric change between wet and dry states), resistance grid sensor, or optical reflectance sensor.

### 1.1.5 Respiration Measurement
- **Definition:** Quantification of CO₂ efflux (dark respiration) or O₂ consumption by plant tissues, expressed in **μmol CO₂ m⁻² s⁻¹**.
- **Measurement Principle:** Infrared Gas Analyzer (IRGA) – measures differential CO₂ concentration in a sealed leaf cuvette.

### 1.1.6 Electromagnetic Radiation (EMR) in Agriculture
- **Definition:** Energy transmitted through space in the form of electromagnetic waves, characterized by wavelength (λ) and frequency (f). The relationship: **c = fλ**, where **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**.
- **Photosynthetically Active Radiation (PAR):** Waveband **400–700 nm**.
- **UV-B:** 280–315 nm (harmful to DNA, but low-dose UV triggers protective flavonoids).
- **Infrared (IR):** 700 nm – 1 mm (thermal emission; used in canopy thermometry).

### 1.1.7 Photosynthesis
- **Definition:** The biochemical process by which plants convert CO₂ + H₂O + light → C₆H₁₂O₆ + O₂. The rate is measured as **Pn (net photosynthesis rate)** in **μmol CO₂ m⁻² s⁻¹**.
- **Two stages:** Light-dependent reactions (in thylakoid membranes) and the Calvin Cycle (in stroma).

### 1.1.8 Infrared (IR) and UV Bio-Sensor Methods
- **IR Bio-Sensor:** Detects thermal emission from canopy (canopy temperature). Uses **thermopile or bolometer** as detector. Key equation: **Stefan-Boltzmann Law: E = εσT⁴**, where **σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W m⁻² K⁻⁴**.
- **UV Bio-Sensor:** Detects UV-induced fluorescence from chlorophyll, or measures UV-B flux using a photomultiplier/semiconductor detector (e.g., SiC photodiode).

---

## 1.2 Weather Stations

**Definition:** An automated agro-meteorological station is an integrated suite of sensors, data loggers, and telemetry that continuously measures atmospheric variables relevant to crop growth, irrigation scheduling, and disease/pest forecasting.

**Conceptual Analogy:** A weather station is the "health monitor" of the sky above the field — much like a patient monitor in an ICU tracks heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation, an agro-weather station tracks temperature, humidity, wind, radiation, and rainfall so the farmer can prescribe irrigation, pesticide, or fertilizer in precise doses.

### Components Overview
- **Air temperature & humidity sensor** (e.g., HMP60, SHT35)
- **Pyranometer** (solar radiation, 400–1100 nm)
- **Anemometer** (wind speed) and **wind vane** (direction)
- **Rain gauge** (tipping bucket / weighing)
- **Barometric pressure sensor**
- **Data logger + telemetry (GSM/LoRa/Satellite)**
- **Leaf wetness & soil moisture sensors** (optional integration)

### Anemometers
- **Cup Anemometer:** Mechanical; rotational speed ∝ wind speed.
- **Sonic Anemometer:** No moving parts; measures wind via ultrasonic transit time differences. Used in research-grade eddy-covariance systems.

### Surface Flux Measurement
- **Definition:** Direct measurement of vertical fluxes of heat (H), water vapor (LE), and CO₂ using the **eddy covariance technique** at frequencies of **10–20 Hz**.
- **Core equation:** $F = \overline{\rho_a \, w' \, \chi'}$ (covariance of vertical wind speed and scalar concentration).

---

## 1.3 Ground Water Occurrence

**Definition:** Groundwater is the water stored in subsurface geological formations (aquifers) within pore spaces and fractures of rocks/sediments.

### 1.3.1 Confined Aquifer
- A water-bearing stratum **trapped between two impermeable layers** (aquicludes/aquitards). Water is under **pressure greater than atmospheric**.
- A well drilled into a confined aquifer produces a **flowing artesian well** if the potentiometric surface is above ground level.

### 1.3.2 Unconfined Aquifer
- The water table is **free to rise and fall** with recharge and discharge. The upper boundary is the **water table** itself; the lower boundary is an impermeable layer.
- Water is at **atmospheric pressure**.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a confined aquifer as a "pressurized water bottle sandwiched between two sheets of solid plastic" — when punctured, water shoots up. An unconfined aquifer is more like a "sponge sitting in a tray of water" — its water level rises and falls with what you pour in or take out.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick-Fact Table:**

| Property | Confined | Unconfined |
|---|---|---|
| Upper boundary | Impermeable layer | Water table (open to atmosphere) |
| Pressure | > atmospheric | = atmospheric |
| Recharge area | Distant | Directly above |
| Hydraulic head | Potentiometric surface | Water table elevation |
| Yield | Higher (artesian) | Lower (pumping lowers table) |

---

## 1.4 Vertical Farming and Hydroponics

**Vertical Farming (VF):** A Controlled Environment Agriculture (CEA) system in which crops are grown in **stacked layers** (vertical towers/shelves) inside warehouses, shipping containers, or purpose-built buildings, using **artificial lighting (LEDs)** and **climate control**.

**Hydroponics:** A soilless cultivation technique where plant roots are supplied with a **nutrient-rich aqueous solution**. The word comes from Greek: *hydro* (water) + *ponos* (work).

**Conceptual Analogy:** Vertical farming is like "skyscraper apartment buildings for plants" — instead of spreading out horizontally across acres of land, the plants are stacked vertically, with each "floor" getting its own light, water, and climate. Hydroponics is the "intravenous drip feed" version of farming — instead of roots digging through soil to find nutrients, the nutrients are delivered directly to the roots in water.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Six major hydroponic systems are: **NFT (Nutrient Film Technique), DWC (Deep Water Culture), Ebb & Flow, Drip System, Aeroponics, and Wick System.** Vertical farming integrates these with **LED lighting (red 660 nm + blue 450 nm ratio)** for photosynthesis optimization.

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Leaf Area Index (LAI) — Theoretical Framework

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. Light interception by a canopy is described by the **Beer-Lambert Law**: $I = I_0 \, e^{-k \cdot \text{LAI}}$
2. The extinction coefficient **k** depends on leaf angle distribution (spherical = 0.5, horizontal = 1.0, vertical = 0.3).
3. LAI can be measured **directly** (destructive sampling, planimeter) or **indirectly** (ceptometer, hemispherical photography, LAI-2200).
4. The indirect optical method uses gap-fraction analysis — measures transmitted light below the canopy and inverts the Beer-Lambert equation.

### KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Plant Growth & Environment

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{LAI} = \dfrac{\text{Total leaf area}}{\text{Ground area}}$ | Leaf area index | dimensionless |
| 2 | $I = I_0 \, e^{-k \cdot \text{LAI}}$ | Beer-Lambert light extinction | $\mu\text{mol m}^{-2}\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 3 | $c = f \lambda$ | Wave-speed relation | m/s |
| 4 | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | Stefan-Boltzmann (IR emission) | W/m² |
| 5 | $E_\lambda = \dfrac{2 \pi h c^2}{\lambda^5} \cdot \dfrac{1}{e^{hc/\lambda k_B T}-1}$ | Planck's law (spectral radiance) | W/m²/sr/nm |
| 6 | $ET_0 = \dfrac{0.408 \, \Delta (R_n - G) + \gamma \dfrac{900}{T+273} u_2 (e_s - e_a)}{\Delta + \gamma (1 + 0.34 u_2)}$ | FAO-56 Penman-Monteith | mm/day |
| 7 | $ET_c = K_c \cdot ET_0$ | Crop ET | mm/day |
| 8 | $\lambda = 2.45$ MJ/kg | Latent heat of vaporization @ 20°C | MJ/kg |
| 9 | $P_n = \dfrac{\Delta CO_2}{\Delta t} \cdot \dfrac{V}{A}$ | Net photosynthesis from IRGA | μmol CO₂ m⁻² s⁻¹ |
| 10 | $F = \overline{\rho_a w' \chi'}$ | Eddy covariance flux | varies |
| 11 | $Q = K \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{dh}{dl}$ | Darcy's law (groundwater) | m³/s |
| 12 | $T = \dfrac{2 L}{v}$ | Ultrasonic transit time | s |
| 13 | $C_d = \dfrac{F_c}{\Delta CO_2}$ | Canopy conductance | m/s |
| 14 | $F = \sigma \cdot T^4 - T_{sky}^4$ | IR radiative net flux | W/m² |
| 15 | $\eta = \dfrac{\mu \text{mol CO}_2 \text{ fixed}}{\mu \text{mol photons absorbed}}$ | Photosynthetic quantum yield | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In all KTU numerical problems, **always** quote units. A numerical answer with units carries full marks; the same number without units is considered incomplete.

## 2.2 Why These Formulas Matter in Real Engineering Practice

- **LAI from Beer-Lambert (Eq. 2):** Used by satellite-based remote sensing (e.g., MODIS LAI/FPAR product) to monitor global vegetation health. Engineers use this for **precision agriculture variable-rate fertilizer maps**.
- **FAO-56 Penman-Monteith (Eq. 6):** Standard tool for **irrigation scheduling** worldwide. The KTU model paper regularly asks students to substitute values for T, RH, u₂, and Rn to compute ET₀.
- **Stefan-Boltzmann (Eq. 4):** Used in **canopy temperature-based irrigation** — the **Crop Water Stress Index (CWSI)** uses canopy-air temperature differential normalized against wet/dry reference surfaces.
- **Eddy Covariance (Eq. 10):** The gold-standard method for **CO₂ flux and water vapor flux** measurement in flux towers globally (FLUXNET network).

## 2.3 Photosynthesis — Light and Dark Reactions

**Light-dependent reactions** (occur in thylakoid membranes):
- Photophosphorylation (cyclic & non-cyclic)
- Water splitting: $2H_2O \rightarrow O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^-$
- NADP⁺ → NADPH; ADP → ATP

**Light-independent reactions (Calvin cycle, in stroma):**
- Carbon fixation by **RuBisCO**: $RuBP + CO_2 \rightarrow 2 \times 3-PGA$
- Reduction phase: 3-PGA → G3P (uses ATP and NADPH)
- Regeneration: G3P → RuBP

**Net reaction:**
$$6 CO_2 + 6 H_2 O \xrightarrow{light, chlorophyll} C_6 H_{12} O_6 + 6 O_2$$

## 2.4 Anemometer Working Principles

### Cup Anemometer
- 3 or 4 hemispherical cups mounted on horizontal arms rotating about a vertical axis.
- Wind force on concave vs convex cup face produces torque; angular velocity $\omega \propto u$ (linearized form: $u = a + b \omega$).
- **Inaccuracy:** Over-rotation in turbulent flow (overspeeding ~10%).

### Sonic Anemometer
- Uses pairs of ultrasonic transducers A and B separated by distance L.
- Transit time in direction A→B: $t_1 = L / (c + u_L)$
- Transit time in direction B→A: $t_2 = L / (c - u_L)$
- The **time difference** $\Delta t = t_1 - t_2$ gives the wind component along the path: $u_L = \dfrac{L}{2} \left( \dfrac{1}{t_1} - \dfrac{1}{t_2} \right)$
- Three orthogonal paths → three components $(u, v, w)$ of wind vector.
- **Sampling rate:** 10–20 Hz (essential for eddy covariance).

## 2.5 Groundwater — Darcy's Law & Aquifer Parameters

$$Q = -K \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{dh}{dl} = K \cdot A \cdot i$$

Where:
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/s)
- $K$ = hydraulic conductivity (m/s)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area (m²)
- $i = dh/dl$ = hydraulic gradient (dimensionless)

**Transmissivity:** $T = K \cdot b$ (where $b$ is aquifer thickness), in **m²/s**.
**Storativity (S):** Volume of water released per unit area per unit decline in head — dimensionless, ranges **0.01–0.30** for unconfined, **10⁻⁵–10⁻³** for confined.

## 2.6 Hydroponics — Nutrient Solution Parameters

A complete hydroponic nutrient solution (e.g., Hoagland's) must supply:
- **Macronutrients:** N, P, K, Ca, Mg, S
- **Micronutrients:** Fe, Mn, Zn, Cu, B, Mo, Cl
- **pH range:** 5.5 – 6.5
- **EC (Electrical Conductivity):** 1.2 – 2.5 mS/cm (lettuce), 2.5 – 3.5 mS/cm (tomato)
- **Dissolved Oxygen (DO):** > 5 mg/L (critical for DWC roots)

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Sonic Anemometer Wind Speed from Transit Times

**Starting Physics:** Sound travels at speed $c$ relative to the air. If the wind has a component $u_L$ along the path of length $L$, the effective speed of sound along the path is $(c + u_L)$ downstream and $(c - u_L)$ upstream.

**Step 1:** Write the transit time in each direction.
$$t_1 = \frac{L}{c + u_L} \quad \text{(downstream)}$$

$$t_2 = \frac{L}{c - u_L} \quad \text{(upstream)}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the **inverse** of each transit time (since $u_L \ll c$, we can expand).
$$\frac{1}{t_1} = \frac{c + u_L}{L}, \qquad \frac{1}{t_2} = \frac{c - u_L}{L}$$

**Step 3:** Subtract to isolate $u_L$.
$$\frac{1}{t_1} - \frac{1}{t_2} = \frac{(c + u_L) - (c - u_L)}{L} = \frac{2 u_L}{L}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $u_L$.
$$u_L = \frac{L}{2} \left( \frac{1}{t_1} - \frac{1}{t_2} \right)$$

**Step 5:** For three orthogonal paths, the **wind vector** $\vec{V} = (u, v, w)$ is obtained by coordinate transformation:
$$\begin{pmatrix} u \\ v \\ w \end{pmatrix} = \mathbf{M}^{-1} \begin{pmatrix} u_{L1} \\ u_{L2} \\ u_{L3} \end{pmatrix}$$

where $\mathbf{M}$ is the geometry-dependent transform matrix.

**Sanity check:** If $u_L = 0$ (no wind), then $t_1 = t_2 = L/c$, giving $u_L = 0$. ✔

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Eddy Covariance CO₂ Flux

**Starting Principle:** The vertical turbulent flux of a scalar $\chi$ (e.g., CO₂ mixing ratio) is the time-averaged covariance with the vertical wind speed $w$.

**Step 1:** Decompose instantaneous variables into mean and turbulent parts (Reynolds decomposition):
$$w = \overline{w} + w', \quad \chi = \overline{\chi} + \chi'$$

**Step 2:** The instantaneous vertical flux density is $F_{inst} = \rho_a \, w \, \chi$.

**Step 3:** Average over the averaging period (typically 30 min). Assume $\overline{w} = 0$ (stationary, horizontal homogeneity), and $\overline{\chi'} = 0$:
$$F = \overline{\rho_a \, w \, \chi} = \rho_a \, \overline{(\overline{w} + w')(\overline{\chi} + \chi')}$$

$$F = \rho_a \, \overline{w' \chi'}$$

**Step 4:** Add the mean advective correction (WPL correction, Webb-Pearman-Leuning) for density fluctuations:
$$F_c = \overline{w' \chi_c'} + \frac{m_a}{m_v} \cdot \frac{\overline{w' \chi_v'}}{\overline{\chi_v}} \cdot \overline{\chi_c}$$

The second term corrects for the fact that heat and water vapor fluctuations cause apparent CO₂ fluctuations even if no real CO₂ is exchanged.

**KTU Tip:** Numerical problems usually present simplified data: $w'$ and $\chi'$ time series; you compute the covariance using $\overline{w' \chi'} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum w'_i \chi'_i$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — Penman-Monteith ET₀

**Problem:** Compute reference evapotranspiration $ET_0$ using the FAO-56 equation for a location with:
- $T = 25°C$
- $RH = 60\%$
- $R_n = 12$ MJ/m²/day
- $G = 0$ (daily, soil heat flux negligible)
- $u_2 = 2$ m/s
- Elevation = 100 m (so atmospheric pressure ≈ 100 kPa)

**Step 1: Saturation vapor pressure $e_s$**
$$e_s(T) = 0.6108 \cdot \exp\left( \frac{17.27 T}{T + 237.3} \right)$$

$$e_s(25) = 0.6108 \cdot \exp\left( \frac{17.27 \times 25}{25 + 237.3} \right) = 0.6108 \cdot \exp(1.6472) = 0.6108 \times 5.190 = 3.171 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2: Actual vapor pressure $e_a$**
$$e_a = \frac{RH}{100} \cdot e_s = 0.60 \times 3.171 = 1.903 \text{ kPa}$$

$$(e_s - e_a) = 3.171 - 1.903 = 1.268 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3: Slope of saturation vapor pressure curve $\Delta$**
$$\Delta = \frac{4098 \cdot e_s}{(T + 237.3)^2} = \frac{4098 \times 3.171}{(25 + 237.3)^2} = \frac{12993}{68748} = 0.1890 \text{ kPa/°C}$$

**Step 4: Psychrometric constant $\gamma$**
$$\gamma = 0.665 \times 10^{-3} \cdot P = 0.665 \times 10^{-3} \times 100 = 0.0665 \text{ kPa/°C}$$

**Step 5: Substitute into FAO-56 PM**
$$ET_0 = \frac{0.408 \cdot \Delta (R_n - G) + \gamma \cdot \frac{900}{T + 273} \cdot u_2 \cdot (e_s - e_a)}{\Delta + \gamma(1 + 0.34 u_2)}$$

**Numerator:**
$$0.408 \times 0.1890 \times 12 = 0.925 \text{ mm/day}$$

$$0.0665 \times \frac{900}{298} \times 2 \times 1.268 = 0.0665 \times 3.020 \times 2 \times 1.268 = 0.509 \text{ mm/day}$$

$$\text{Numerator total} = 0.925 + 0.509 = 1.434 \text{ mm/day}$$

**Denominator:**
$$\Delta + \gamma(1 + 0.34 \cdot 2) = 0.1890 + 0.0665 \times 1.68 = 0.1890 + 0.1117 = 0.3007 \text{ kPa/°C}$$

**Final result:**
$$ET_0 = \frac{1.434}{0.3007} = 4.77 \text{ mm/day}$$

**Verification:** A reference ET of ~4.8 mm/day is realistic for a warm, moderately windy, semi-humid day. ✔

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Numerical Trap:** The constant **900** in FAO-56 PM is for **daily** ET with $u_2$ at 2 m. For **hourly**, the constant is **1600**. KTU exam questions always specify the timestep — read it carefully!

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Beer-Lambert Light Interception

**Problem:** A wheat canopy has LAI = 4.0, with extinction coefficient $k = 0.5$ (spherical leaf distribution). The incident PAR above the canopy is $I_0 = 1000 \; \mu \text{mol m}^{-2} \text{s}^{-1}$. Find the PAR reaching the soil surface.

**Solution:**
$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot \text{LAI}} = 1000 \cdot e^{-0.5 \times 4.0} = 1000 \cdot e^{-2.0}$$

$$e^{-2.0} = 0.1353$$

$$I = 1000 \times 0.1353 = 135 \; \mu \text{mol m}^{-2} \text{s}^{-1}$$

**Interpretation:** Only ~13.5% of the incoming PAR reaches the soil — the canopy is highly efficient at light interception. This justifies why **higher LAI is desirable** in productive crops (up to the optimum).

## PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define Leaf Area Index (LAI) and state the Beer-Lambert equation governing light transmission through a plant canopy.

**Model Answer:**
**Leaf Area Index (LAI):** It is defined as the total one-sided projected leaf area per unit ground area. Mathematically, $\text{LAI} = \dfrac{A_{\text{leaf}}}{A_{\text{ground}}}$, and is dimensionless. Typical crop canopies have LAI values between 1 and 6.

**Beer-Lambert Equation for canopy light transmission:**
$$I = I_0 \, e^{-k \cdot \text{LAI}}$$
where $I_0$ is the incident PAR above the canopy, $I$ is the PAR reaching a given depth, and $k$ is the extinction coefficient (0.3 for vertical leaves, 0.5 for spherical, 1.0 for horizontal).

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Distinguish between a confined and unconfined aquifer.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Unconfined Aquifer | Confined Aquifer |
|---|---|---|
| Upper boundary | Open to atmosphere (water table) | Impermeable layer (aquitard) |
| Pressure | Atmospheric | Greater than atmospheric |
| Water table | Fluctuates with recharge | Replaced by potentiometric surface |
| Recharge area | Directly above aquifer | Distant, requires leakage |
| Yield type | Water table well | Artesian / flowing well |
| Storativity | 0.01 – 0.30 | 0.00001 – 0.001 |

1. **Pyranometer (Solar Radiation):** 0–2000 W/m², accuracy ±3%, spectral 400–1100 nm, thermopile-based.
2. **Quantum Sensor (PAR):** 0–2500 μmol m⁻² s⁻¹, accuracy ±5%, 400–700 nm filtered silicon photodiode.
3. **Air Temperature & Humidity Probe:** T = -40 to +60 °C (±0.3 °C), RH = 0–100% (±2%), capacitive polymer sensor.
4. **Cup Anemometer:** 0–75 m/s, accuracy ±0.5 m/s, three-cup rotating assembly.
5. **Wind Vane:** 0–360°, accuracy ±3°, potentiometric or encoder output.
6. **Tipping Bucket Rain Gauge:** Resolution 0.2 mm, accuracy ±2%, funnels rain into a calibrated tipping bucket.
7. **Barometric Pressure Sensor:** 500–1100 hPa, accuracy ±0.5 hPa, MEMS piezoresistive.
8. **Leaf Wetness Sensor:** Dielectric change detection, output 0 (dry) to 1 (wet), time constant 1–2 min.
9. **Soil Moisture Probe (TDR or Capacitive):** 0–100% VWC, accuracy ±2%.

**Data Logger:** Microcontroller-based, 12–24 bit ADC, 1 Hz to 1 sample/min logging rate, telemetry via GSM/LoRa/satellite, local SD backup.

#### (b) Explain the working principle of a sonic anemometer with a neat diagram. Derive the expression for the wind speed component along the transducer path. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:** A sonic anemometer measures wind speed by determining the difference in ultrasonic transit times between pairs of transducers oriented along three non-coplanar paths. It has **no moving parts** and can sample at 10–20 Hz, making it ideal for **eddy covariance** flux measurements.

**Diagram:**
```
   Transducer A (TX)  ←———— L ————→  Transducer B (RX)
   Transducer B (TX)  ←———— L ————→  Transducer A (RX)
                    (Two-way measurement)
   Three such pairs (orthogonal) → 3D wind (u, v, w)
```

**Derivation (as shown in Section 3.1):**

Let $c$ = speed of sound in still air, $u_L$ = wind component along path, $L$ = path length.

$$t_1 = \frac{L}{c + u_L}, \quad t_2 = \frac{L}{c - u_L}$$

Subtracting the inverses:
$$\frac{1}{t_1} - \frac{1}{t_2} = \frac{2 u_L}{L}$$

$$\boxed{u_L = \frac{L}{2}\left( \frac{1}{t_1} - \frac{1}{t_2} \right)}$$

For three orthogonal paths $(i = 1, 2, 3)$, the wind vector is obtained by geometry-based coordinate transformation. Advantages include **no mechanical inertia, fast response, and direct 3D output**. Limitations include sensitivity to **temperature gradients and precipitation** in the measurement volume.

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

#### (a) Define evapotranspiration. With the FAO-56 Penman-Monteith equation, compute the reference ET (ET₀) for a given day. Given: T = 30°C, RH = 50%, Rn = 14 MJ/m²/day, G = 0, u₂ = 1.8 m/s, P = 101.3 kPa. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Evapotranspiration (ET) is the combined loss of water from the soil surface (evaporation) and from plant tissues (transpiration) to the atmosphere, expressed in mm/day.

**FAO-56 Penman-Monteith Equation:**
$$ET_0 = \frac{0.408 \Delta (R_n - G) + \gamma \dfrac{900}{T+273} u_2 (e_s - e_a)}{\Delta + \gamma(1 + 0.34 u_2)}$$

**Step 1: Saturation vapor pressure $e_s$**
$$e_s(30) = 0.6108 \cdot \exp\left(\frac{17.27 \times 30}{30 + 237.3}\right) = 0.6108 \cdot \exp(1.938) = 0.6108 \times 6.945 = 4.243 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2: Actual vapor pressure $e_a$**
$$e_a = 0.50 \times 4.243 = 2.122 \text{ kPa} \quad \Rightarrow \quad (e_s - e_a) = 2.121 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3: Slope $\Delta$**
$$\Delta = \frac{4098 \times 4.243}{(30 + 237.3)^2} = \frac{17388}{71511} = 0.2432 \text{ kPa/°C}$$

**Step 4: Psychrometric constant $\gamma$**
$$\gamma = 0.665 \times 10^{-3} \times 101.3 = 0.0674 \text{ kPa/°C}$$

**Step 5: Numerator**
$$0.408 \times 0.2432 \times 14 = 1.389 \text{ mm/day}$$

$$0.0674 \times \frac{900}{303} \times 1.8 \times 2.121 = 0.0674 \times 2.970 \times 1.8 \times 2.121 = 0.764 \text{ mm/day}$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 1.389 + 0.764 = 2.153 \text{ mm/day}$$

**Step 6: Denominator**
$$\Delta + \gamma(1 + 0.34 \times 1.8) = 0.2432 + 0.0674 \times 1.612 = 0.2432 + 0.1086 = 0.3518$$

**Step 7: ET₀**
$$ET_0 = \frac{2.153}{0.3518} = \boxed{6.12 \text{ mm/day}}$$

**Note:** A higher value (6.12 vs 4.77) is consistent with the higher temperature and radiation input compared to the example in Section 3.3.

#### (b) Explain the principles of IR and UV bio-sensor methods used in agriculture. Discuss how canopy IR thermometry is used to compute the Crop Water Stress Index (CWSI). (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**IR (Infrared) Bio-Sensor Methods:**

- **Principle:** All objects with temperature above absolute zero emit thermal IR radiation governed by the Stefan-Boltzmann law: $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$, where $\varepsilon$ is emissivity (~0.98 for green vegetation), $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²/K⁴.
- **Detector:** Thermopile or microbolometer that converts incident IR flux into a voltage proportional to the **fourth power of surface temperature**.
- **Agricultural use:** 
   1. **Canopy temperature measurement** for water stress detection.
   2. **Soil surface temperature** mapping.
   3. **Grain temperature** monitoring in silos.
   4. **Livestock/Animal body temperature** remote sensing.

**UV Bio-Sensor Methods:**

- **Principle:** Certain biological and chemical compounds absorb UV (especially UV-B, 280–315 nm) and re-emit at longer wavelengths (UV/visible fluorescence). Examples: chlorophyll fluorescence at 685 nm (red) and 740 nm (far-red).
- **Detector:** Photomultiplier tube (PMT), SiC photodiode, or CCD spectrometer.
- **Agricultural use:** 
   1. **Chlorophyll fluorescence** to assess photosynthetic efficiency ($F_v/F_m$ ratio).
   2. **UV-B dosimetry** to monitor harmful radiation for plant protection.
   3. **Detection of UV-absorbing pesticides/herbicides** in produce.

**Crop Water Stress Index (CWSI) — Derivation:**
$$\text{CWSI} = \frac{(T_{\text{canopy}} - T_{\text{air}})_{\text{measured}} - (T_{\text{canopy}} - T_{\text{air}})_{\text{wet baseline}}}{(T_{\text{canopy}} - T_{\text{air}})_{\text{dry baseline}} - (T_{\text{canopy}} - T_{\text{air}})_{\text{wet baseline}}}$$

- **Wet baseline** (well-watered reference): transpiration cools the canopy → $T_c < T_a$.
- **Dry baseline** (fully stressed, no transpiration): $T_c = T_a + $ solar heating differential.
- **CWSI ≈ 0** → No water stress.
- **CWSI ≈ 1** → Severe water stress.
- A threshold of **CWSI > 0.3** typically triggers irrigation in many field crops.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Missing the latent heat of vaporization constant** (λ = 2.45 MJ/kg) when the question says "convert from MJ/m²/day to mm/day." The unit conversion uses 1 mm ET = 2.45 MJ/m².
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract G** in the FAO-56 PM numerator for hourly calculations (G ≠ 0 for hourly). For daily, G ≈ 0 is acceptable.
> 3. **Confusing wind speed in units** — the FAO-56 PM uses u₂ in **m/s at 2 m height**, not 10 m. If given at 10 m, convert using the logarithmic wind profile: $u_2 = u_{10} \cdot \dfrac{\ln(2/z_0)}{\ln(10/z_0)}$ or use the standard empirical factor $u_2 = u_{10} \times 0.748$ for grassland ($z_0 = 0.01$ m).
> 4. **Confined vs Unconfined aquifer** — students often write "unconfined has a higher pressure than confined" — this is **wrong**. Confined is pressurized; unconfined is at atmospheric.
> 5. **LAI units** — writing LAI in cm² is a **common error**. LAI is dimensionless.
> 6. **In sonic anemometer derivation** — students forget to take **inverse of t** and just subtract $t_1 - t_2$. The correct form is $(1/t_1) - (1/t_2)$.
> 7. **In CWSI** — students write the formula but **fail to define the wet and dry baselines** explicitly. Examiners award at least 1 mark for the baselines alone.

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### A. Plant Growth Monitoring Essentials
- **LAI = Total leaf area / Ground area** (dimensionless, 1–6 typical). Use Beer-Lambert: $I = I_0 e^{-k \cdot \text{LAI}}$, $k = 0.5$ spherical.
- **PAR** = 400–700 nm; **UV-B** = 280–315 nm; **IR** > 700 nm.
- **Net photosynthesis** measured by **IRGA** in a leaf chamber; units **μmol CO₂ m⁻² s⁻¹**.
- **Respiration** = CO₂ efflux in dark; **Photosynthesis** = CO₂ uptake in light.
- **Evapotranspiration** uses **FAO-56 Penman-Monteith**; remember the **0.408** and **900** constants (daily timestep).
- **Crop ET** = **$K_c \times ET_0$**; $K_c$ is the crop coefficient.
- **Leaf wetness** = dielectric-based capacitive sensor; critical for fungal disease prediction.
- **IR thermometry** + canopy temperature = **CWSI** for irrigation scheduling.
- **UV-B detection** = chlorophyll fluorescence or SiC photodiode.

### B. Weather Station Essentials
- Standard sensors: **Pyranometer (W/m²), Quantum (μmol/m²/s), T/RH probe, Anemometer, Wind vane, Rain gauge, Barometer, Leaf wetness**.
- **Cup anemometer** has moving parts; **sonic anemometer** has none and gives 3D wind.
- **Sonic formula:** $u_L = \frac{L}{2}\left(\frac{1}{t_1} - \frac{1}{t_2}\right)$.
- **Eddy covariance flux:** $F = \rho_a \overline{w' \chi'}$; sample at 10–20 Hz; apply WPL density correction.

### C. Groundwater Essentials
- **Unconfined aquifer** = open water table, atmospheric pressure, storativity 0.01–0.30.
- **Confined aquifer** = bounded by two aquitards, pressurized (artesian), storativity $10^{-5}$–$10^{-3}$.
- **Darcy's Law:** $Q = -K A (dh/dl) = K A i$.
- **Transmissivity** $T = K b$; **Specific yield (unconfined)** vs **Storativity (confined)**.
- **Recharge area** of confined aquifer is **distant**; of unconfined is **directly above**.

### D. Vertical Farming & Hydroponics Essentials
- **Vertical farming** = stacked, indoor, **LED-lit** crops in **CEA**.
- **Six hydroponic systems:** NFT, DWC, Ebb & Flow, Drip, Aeroponics, Wick.
- **LED ratio:** Red 660 nm + Blue 450 nm drives photosynthesis; Red:Blue ≈ 3:1 for leafy greens.
- **Hoagland's solution** = standard reference nutrient recipe; pH 5.5–6.5; EC varies by crop.
- **DO > 5 mg/L** in DWC; **TDS / EC** monitored continuously.
- **Closed-loop** hydroponics recirculates water → saves up to **90% water** vs open-field.
- **Advantages:** Year-round production, pesticide-free, no soil-borne diseases, urban deployment.
- **Challenges:** High capital cost, energy for LEDs, single point of failure in nutrient/pH.

### E. Mnemonics & Quick Memory Aids
- **"LIGHT-C**" for EMR in agriculture: **L**ong-wave IR, **I**nfrared, **G**reen (500–570 nm), **H**eat (IR), **T**hermal, **C**osmic.
- **"PAN"** for photosynthesis inputs: **P**AR + **A**ir (CO₂) + **N**utrients/water.
- **"N-D-C"** for KTU aquifer comparison: **N**on-pressure (unconfined), **D**istant recharge (confined), **C**onfined gives artesian.
- **"FAIR-W"** for weather station sensors: **F**lux (pyranometer), **A**ir T/RH, **I**nclination (rain), **R**adiation quantum, **W**ind.

### F. Numerical Constants to Memorize
- $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²/K⁴
- $\lambda = 2.45$ MJ/kg (latent heat at 20°C)
- 1 mm ET/day = 2.45 MJ/m²/day
- 900 (daily) / 1600 (hourly) FAO-56 constants
- 0.408 = $1/\lambda$ conversion factor in FAO-56
- 0.665 × 10⁻³ = psychrometric constant coefficient
- $K_c$ ranges: 0.3 (initial), 1.15 (mid-season, alfalfa = 1.0), 0.5 (late)
